posts.title: GRPO: Reinforcement Learning Without a Critic Model
posts.subtitle: Skip the value network — score a group of sampled answers against each other and turn the ranking into a training signal.
posts.summary: GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization), introduced in DeepSeek's DeepSeekMath paper and later the backbone of DeepSeek-R1's reasoning training, drops PPO's learned critic model entirely. Instead, it samples a group of responses to the same prompt and normalizes each one's reward against the group's own mean and standard deviation — a model-free baseline that halves the memory footprint of RLHF-style training and has since spread into 2026's agentic-RL pipelines.

slides.title: Training LLMs to Reason Without a Second Model to Judge Them
slides.body: Classic RLHF (PPO) needs four models in memory at once: the policy, a frozen reference copy, a reward model, and a critic/value model that estimates how good a partial answer is. GRPO, introduced in DeepSeek's DeepSeekMath paper (Feb 2024), drops the critic entirely.
bullets: PPO's value/critic model is roughly the same size as the policy — for a 70B-parameter policy, that's another ~70B-parameter model just to estimate advantages
GRPO replaces it with a simple idea: sample a group of answers to the same prompt, and use their relative scores as the advantage signal
It's the RL algorithm behind DeepSeek-R1's reasoning training and is now widely reused across 2026's agentic-RL pipelines

slides.title: Why PPO Is Expensive for LLM Post-Training
slides.body: Standard PPO computes an advantage per token by subtracting a learned value estimate from the observed reward — and that value model is itself expensive and unstable to train.
bullets: Four models in memory during training: policy, frozen reference (for the KL penalty), reward model, and critic — each roughly policy-sized at LLM scale
The critic is trained on a moving target (the same policy it's judging), a well-known source of PPO instability
For math, coding, and reasoning tasks with a clear right answer, a learned per-token value estimate is solving a harder problem than the task actually needs

slides.title: The Group Is the Baseline
slides.body: For each prompt, GRPO samples a group of G responses (e.g. G=16) from the current policy, scores each with the reward function, and normalizes each response's reward against the group's own mean and standard deviation.
bullets: Advantage for response i = (reward_i − mean(group rewards)) / std(group rewards) — no learned value model required
Responses better than the group average get reinforced; responses below average get suppressed, all relative to what the current policy can already do on that exact prompt
A KL penalty against a frozen reference policy (same as PPO) keeps updates from drifting too far in a single step

slides.title: From Math Proofs to Agentic Tool Use
slides.body: GRPO's simplicity — no critic, one reward call per sampled response — is exactly what made it easy to reuse outside its original math-reasoning setting.
bullets: DeepSeekMath used it to push math reasoning without extra critic infrastructure; DeepSeek-R1 later used the same core algorithm to train chain-of-thought reasoning at scale
2026's agentic-RL work builds on GRPO's group-relative-advantage idea for multi-turn, tool-using agents, not just single-turn math answers

It composes well with verifiable rewards (unit tests pass/fail, a math answer checker) because the group-normalized signal doesn't need the reward to be smooth or dense — a binary pass/fail per sample is enough

slides.title: GRPO vs. PPO vs. DPO

rows: General RLHF, works off single rollouts
Reasoning RL with a verifiable or scorable reward (math, code)
No — no RL loop at all
No — trains on a fixed preference pair
Preference fine-tuning from an existing labeled/ranked dataset

slides.title: Worked Example: Training on a Single Math Prompt
slides.body: A simplified walk-through with a small group (real runs typically use larger groups, e.g. G=16 or more).
bullets: Prompt: "What is the derivative of x³ + 2x?" The policy samples 4 chain-of-thought answers; a rule-based checker scores each 1 (correct final answer) or 0 (incorrect)
Say the scores are [1, 1, 0, 0] → group mean = 0.5. The two correct answers get positive normalized advantage, the two incorrect ones get negative advantage
The policy is updated to make the correct-answer token sequences more likely and the incorrect ones less likely — purely from this one prompt's internal comparison, no separate value model needed

slides.title: Where GRPO Struggles
slides.body: Dropping the critic isn't free — the cost moves elsewhere.
bullets: Needs a reward that's cheap and reliable to compute many times per prompt (G samples × prompts per batch) — an expensive or noisy reward model erodes the benefit of dropping the critic

If every sampled response in a group gets the same score (all correct or all wrong), the group's standard deviation is near zero and the advantage signal collapses toward nothing for that prompt — wasted compute
Group sampling multiplies inference cost per training step (G rollouts instead of 1) even though it removes the critic — the savings are in memory and stability, not necessarily total compute

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: GRPO replaces PPO's learned critic with a model-free baseline: sample a group of responses to the same prompt and normalize each one's reward against the group's own mean and standard deviation

It cuts the memory footprint of RLHF-style training (policy + frozen reference, no separate critic) while working especially well with clear-cut, verifiable rewards like math correctness or passing tests
It's the algorithm behind DeepSeekMath and DeepSeek-R1's reasoning training, and its group-relative-advantage idea has since been extended into 2026's agentic and multi-turn RL work
The catch: it needs a cheap reward you can call G times per prompt, and groups where every sample scores identically waste the compute spent sampling them

questions.prompt: What does GRPO use instead of a learned critic/value model to compute the advantage for a sampled response?
options: A second, larger frozen model trained purely to imitate human raters
The response's reward normalized against the mean and standard deviation of a group of responses sampled for the same prompt
A hand-coded heuristic based on response length
Nothing — GRPO has no advantage signal at all
questions.explanation: GRPO samples a group of G responses per prompt, scores each with the reward function, and computes each one's advantage as (reward − group mean) / group std — no separate value network needed.

questions.prompt: Why does PPO typically need more memory than GRPO for the same policy size?
options: PPO requires 4x more GPUs for no algorithmic reason
PPO trains a separate critic/value model roughly the size of the policy itself, plus the reward model and frozen reference, while GRPO drops the critic
GRPO uses a smaller reward model than PPO
PPO doesn't support KL penalties
questions.explanation: PPO keeps a policy, frozen reference, reward model, and critic in memory — the critic alone is typically policy-sized. GRPO removes the critic and gets its baseline from the sampled group instead.

questions.prompt: What happens to GRPO's training signal for a prompt where every sampled response in the group gets the exact same reward?
options: The policy update becomes larger to compensate
Nothing changes, this case doesn't affect training
The group's standard deviation is near zero, so the normalized advantage collapses toward zero — that prompt contributes little to the update despite the sampling cost
GRPO automatically resamples with a larger group
questions.explanation: Advantage is reward normalized by the group's standard deviation. If every sample in the group scores identically (all correct or all wrong), there's no within-group signal to learn from — the compute spent sampling that group is largely wasted.

references.title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models

posts.title: Quantization: Squeezing a 70B Model Onto One GPU
posts.subtitle: Round the weights to fewer bits, protect the ones that matter, and keep almost all the accuracy.
posts.summary: Post-training quantization stores each weight in fewer bits (16 to 8 to 4) to cut a model's memory footprint, but naive uniform rounding wrecks accuracy because real weight distributions have outliers. GPTQ fixes this with calibration-driven, sequential per-column correction; AWQ instead rescales the small fraction of activation-salient weight channels before rounding. Both can push a 70B model from ~140GB down to ~35GB with only a small accuracy cost — but push past 3 bits and quality typically falls off a cliff.

slides.title: A 70B Model Doesn't Fit on Your GPU
slides.body: Every parameter in a transformer is normally stored as a 16-bit float. That precision is nearly free during training, but at inference it's mostly wasted — and it's the single biggest cost of serving a large model.
bullets: A 70B-parameter model in fp16 needs roughly 140GB just for the weights, before activations or a KV cache
That's more memory than two 80GB GPUs, before serving a single token
Quantization asks: what if each weight were stored in 8, 4, or even fewer bits instead of 16?

slides.title: Naive Rounding Breaks the Model
slides.body: You can't just round every weight uniformly to a lower bit-width and call it done — real transformer weight distributions have long tails, and the largest values dominate the error.
bullets: Uniform (linear) quantization maps a weight's range onto a fixed number of buckets — 4 bits gives only 16 distinct levels for the entire range
A handful of outlier weights or activation channels stretch that range, so most 'normal' weights get crushed into just a few buckets and lose precision
Naive INT4 on an untouched model can cost several points of accuracy and send perplexity climbing — enough to make outputs noticeably worse

slides.title: GPTQ: Quantize Layer-by-Layer, Correct as You Go
slides.body: GPTQ (Frantar et al., 2022) treats quantization as an optimization problem, not a rounding rule.
bullets: It runs a small calibration dataset through the model and uses the resulting activation statistics to approximate each layer's Hessian — how sensitive the output is to each weight
Weights are quantized one column at a time; after each column, the remaining unquantized weights in that layer are nudged to compensate for the error just introduced
This sequential correction is what lets GPTQ push to 4 bits (sometimes 3) with only a small accuracy hit, without any retraining or gradient descent

slides.title: AWQ: Protect the ~1% of Weights That Actually Matter
slides.body: AWQ (Lin et al., 2023) skips the heavy Hessian math and asks a simpler question: which weight channels see the largest activations?
bullets: It observes activations on a small calibration set and finds the small fraction of weight channels — often around 1% — tied to consistently large activation magnitudes
Instead of leaving those channels at full precision, AWQ rescales them before quantizing, shrinking their rounding error without complicating the compute graph

No backpropagation and no per-layer reconstruction loop, so AWQ's calibration pass is markedly faster than GPTQ's — a practical reason it's a common default in serving stacks like vLLM and TensorRT-LLM

slides.title: Precision vs. Memory: The Direct Tradeoff

rows: Safe middle ground, minimal quality loss
Single high-memory GPU serving, near-fp16 quality

slides.title: Worked Example: Serving a 70B Chat Model on One GPU
slides.body: Concrete scenario: you want to serve a 70B-class chat model for a small team, and you have exactly one 48GB GPU.
bullets: At FP16 (~140GB) or even INT8 (~70GB), the model alone doesn't fit — you'd need multi-GPU tensor parallelism just to hold the weights
Quantize to 4-bit with AWQ or GPTQ: weights shrink to roughly 35GB, leaving headroom on a 48GB card for the KV cache and activations of several concurrent requests
The tradeoff you accept: slightly higher perplexity and a small per-multiply dequantization cost — usually a good trade for turning 'impossible on this hardware' into 'running today'

slides.title: When Quantization Bites Back
slides.body: Quantization is a real accuracy-for-memory trade, not a free win — and it doesn't fail evenly.
bullets: Below about 3 bits, quality typically falls off a cliff rather than degrading gracefully — most production deployments stop at 4 bits for exactly this reason
A calibration set that doesn't match real traffic (wrong language, wrong domain) can misidentify 'salient' channels, quietly hurting accuracy on exactly the inputs you care about
Quantizing weights alone doesn't reduce FLOPs — without kernels that natively compute in low precision, you save memory and bandwidth but not necessarily wall-clock compute
Some layers (often the first and last) are more sensitive than others; naively quantizing everything uniformly is why tooling like GGUF's K-quants supports mixed per-layer precision

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Quantization stores weights in fewer bits (16 to 8 to 4) to cut memory roughly in half at each step, making large models fit on smaller hardware
GPTQ uses calibration-derived Hessian approximations and sequential per-column correction to reach 4-bit with minimal accuracy loss, no retraining required
AWQ instead identifies and rescales the small fraction of activation-salient weight channels — faster to calibrate, no backprop, popular in serving frameworks
Below roughly 3 bits, accuracy drops sharply, and quantization saves memory/bandwidth more reliably than raw compute — pick your bit-width based on the hardware constraint you actually have

questions.prompt: Why can't you just uniformly round every weight in a transformer to 4 bits?
options: Transformers don't support integer math at all
Real weight distributions have outliers that stretch the range, crushing most 'normal' values into very few of the 16 available buckets
4-bit numbers can only represent even numbers
GPUs cannot store integers, only floats
questions.explanation: With 4 bits you only get 16 distinct levels to cover the whole range. Outlier weights stretch that range so most other weights collapse into just a few of those levels — which is why naive uniform rounding costs noticeable accuracy, and why methods like GPTQ and AWQ exist.

questions.prompt: What does GPTQ use to decide how to quantize each layer?
options: A fixed rounding rule applied identically to every model
Calibration-data-derived Hessian approximations, quantizing one column at a time and adjusting the remaining weights to compensate for the error just introduced
A second neural network trained to predict quantized weights
Random search over possible bit-widths
questions.explanation: GPTQ runs a calibration set through the model to approximate each layer's Hessian, then quantizes weights column-by-column, updating the not-yet-quantized weights after each step to correct for the rounding error — all without gradient descent or retraining.

questions.prompt: How does AWQ differ from GPTQ in what it protects?
options: AWQ quantizes every weight to a lower bit-width than GPTQ
AWQ identifies the small fraction of weight channels tied to large activation magnitudes and rescales them before quantizing, instead of computing a full Hessian-based correction
AWQ only works for 8-bit quantization, never 4-bit
AWQ requires full retraining of the model
questions.explanation: AWQ skips GPTQ's Hessian approximation and per-layer reconstruction loop. It instead finds the small share of activation-salient weight channels and rescales them to shrink their rounding error — a lighter calibration pass that's become a common default in serving stacks like vLLM.

references.title: GPTQ: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for Generative Pre-trained Transformers
AWQ: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for LLM Compression and Acceleration

posts.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration: When One Agent Isn't Enough
posts.subtitle: Supervisor, swarm, or debate — the pattern you pick decides whether more agents means more accuracy or just more cost
posts.summary: Multi-agent orchestration splits a task across several LLM agents instead of one — a supervisor delegating to specialists, a swarm working in parallel, or agents debating each other. Each pattern trades latency, cost, and coordination complexity differently, and the wrong pattern can multiply spend without multiplying quality.

slides.title: More Agents Isn't Automatically Better
slides.body: A single LLM call hits real limits on long, heterogeneous, or parallelizable tasks. Multi-agent orchestration splits the work across several agent instances — but the coordination pattern you choose matters as much as the split itself.
bullets: Used underneath deep-research tools, coding assistants with subagents, and enterprise workflow agents
The core question every pattern answers differently: who decides what each agent does, and who reconciles their outputs?

slides.title: Where a Single Agent Breaks Down
slides.body: One agent, one context window, one thread of reasoning — that model runs out of room well before real tasks do.
bullets: Context dilution: cramming a 10-step research task with 6 different tool schemas into one prompt degrades attention to any single part
No true parallelism: a single agent's tool calls are typically serialized inside one reasoning trace, so independent subtasks still run one after another
Role conflict: an agent that both drafts an answer and critiques it tends to be a lenient critic of its own work
Blast radius: one bad tool call or hallucinated fact can derail the entire remaining trajectory with nothing to catch it

slides.title: Three Core Coordination Patterns
slides.body: Almost every multi-agent system reduces to one of these shapes, or a mix.
bullets: Orchestrator-worker: a supervisor agent decomposes the task, dispatches subtasks to specialist workers, and synthesizes their results — the most common production pattern
Parallel swarm: identical or near-identical agents attack independent slices of the same problem simultaneously, then a merge step combines outputs
Debate / critic-actor: two or more agents argue different positions or one agent generates while another adversarially reviews, forcing errors to surface before the final answer ships

slides.title: The Orchestrator-Worker Loop in Practice
slides.body: The supervisor never does the work itself — it plans, delegates, and reconciles.
bullets: 1) Supervisor reads the task and breaks it into independent or sequential subtasks
2) Each subtask goes to a worker agent with only the context it needs — not the full conversation history
3) Workers run with their own tool access and return a bounded, structured result (not a raw transcript)
4) Supervisor reconciles results, resolves conflicts between workers, and produces the final output
Isolating each worker's context is the key win: a worker researching 'pricing' never sees the noise from the worker researching 'competitors'

slides.title: Worked Example: Comparing Three Vendors
slides.body: Task: 'Compare Vendor A, B, and C on pricing, SLAs, and integration effort, then recommend one.'
bullets: Supervisor spawns 3 worker agents in parallel, one per vendor, each with the same research tools but a scoped instruction
Each worker independently gathers pricing, SLA terms, and integration notes for just its vendor — no cross-contamination of facts
Workers return structured findings (not free-form essays), which the supervisor merges into one comparison
Supervisor makes the final recommendation, weighing the three structured results against the original ask
Wall-clock time: roughly one research round-trip, not three sequential ones — the reason to parallelize in the first place

slides.title: Pick the Pattern by What You're Optimizing For

rows: N x token cost, needs a merge step
Duplicate or contradictory outputs at merge
Critic can rubber-stamp instead of truly challenging

slides.title: Tradeoffs and Pitfalls
slides.body: Orchestration buys accuracy and parallelism, but never for free.
bullets: Cost multiplies, not adds: a 3-worker swarm is roughly 3x the token spend of one agent, before counting the supervisor's own calls
Coordination overhead is real work: writing subtask instructions, structured result schemas, and conflict resolution logic is often harder than the task itself
Errors can compound across agents just as easily as within one — a worker that hallucinates a fact can poison the supervisor's synthesis if nothing checks it
Skip orchestration when: the task is short, has no independent subtasks, or a single well-scoped agent already solves it reliably — added agents just add latency and cost with no accuracy gain

slides.title: Match the Shape of the Task to the Shape of the System
slides.body: The orchestration pattern should mirror how the task actually decomposes — not be bolted on because 'agents' sounds more capable.
bullets: Sequential, dependent steps → orchestrator-worker with a supervisor that passes results forward
Independent, similarly-shaped subtasks → parallel swarm for wall-clock speed
High-stakes or ambiguous correctness → debate/critic pattern to force adversarial review
Always isolate each worker's context to just what it needs — shared full-history context is the fastest way to blow the cost budget
Default to a single agent first; add orchestration only when a concrete bottleneck (context, parallelism, or role conflict) demands it

questions.prompt: In the orchestrator-worker pattern, why does each worker typically get only a scoped slice of context instead of the full conversation history?
options: Workers are not allowed to use tools
Isolating context avoids diluting the worker's attention with irrelevant history and prevents cross-contamination between subtasks
It's a hard technical limit of all LLM APIs
The supervisor cannot generate more than one prompt
questions.explanation: A worker researching one vendor doesn't need the noise from another vendor's research thread. Scoped context keeps each worker focused and keeps token spend down, which is the main practical reason to isolate context per worker.

questions.prompt: A parallel swarm of 4 agents working the same task roughly costs how much compared to a single agent, before the merge step?
options: The same, since LLM calls are billed per task not per agent
Roughly 4x the token cost, since each agent runs its own full reasoning pass
Always cheaper, because parallel calls share a context window
Exactly half, due to caching
questions.explanation: Cost multiplies with agent count in a swarm — 4 independent agents each running their own reasoning and tool calls is roughly 4x the token spend of one agent, which is the central tradeoff against the wall-clock speedup.

questions.prompt: When should you skip multi-agent orchestration and use a single agent instead?
options: Whenever the task involves any tool calls at all
When the task is short, has no independent subtasks, and a single well-scoped agent already handles it reliably
Never — multi-agent systems always outperform single agents
Only when working with open-source models
questions.explanation: Orchestration adds real coordination overhead, latency, and multiplied cost. If a task doesn't decompose into independent or clearly separable subtasks, adding agents just adds expense without an accuracy or speed payoff.

posts.title: Agents' Last Exam: Where Frontier Agents Score 0%
posts.subtitle: Berkeley's new benchmark trades toy tasks for 1,500+ real jobs — and top agents still can't clear the hardest tier.
posts.summary: Agents' Last Exam (ALE) is a Berkeley RDI benchmark built from 1,500+ real professional tasks across 55 industries. Frontier agents average 26% overall but collapse to near 0% on the hardest tier — even models that score 82% on Terminal-Bench.

slides.title: Built to Break Benchmark Saturation
bullets: Berkeley RDI + 300 industry experts, published June 2026 (arXiv 2606.05405)
1,500+ tasks across 55 sub-industries, drawn from real professional work
Frontier agents: ~26% overall, but near 0% on the hardest tier

slides.title: Existing Benchmarks Were Topping Out
slides.body: Models were clearing 80%+ on benchmarks like Terminal-Bench, but that stopped tracking whether an agent could actually do a client's job.
bullets: Codex with GPT-5.5 scores 82% on Terminal-Bench — a benchmark widely treated as 'solved'
ALE asks a harder question: does that translate into real, deliverable work?
300+ professionals (Berkeley, MIT, Morgan Stanley, Adobe, AstraZeneca) contributed tasks that took them days or weeks to complete originally

slides.title: Real Sandboxes, Deterministic Grading
bullets: Agents get one task description and an unconstrained real OS sandbox — no scaffolding hints
Final artifacts are graded by deterministic, reproducible code evaluators, not human judges
Tasks span 55 sub-industries in 13 clusters, mapped to the U.S. O*NET/SOC occupational taxonomy
Three difficulty tiers: near-term, full-spectrum, and last-exam

slides.title: Solved Benchmark vs. Real Task
slides.body: Same model, wildly different scores depending on whether the task looks like a benchmark or looks like a job.

slides.title: Tasks Look Like Client Work, Not Trivia
slides.body: There's no multiple-choice version of ALE — every task is a real deliverable pulled from an actual job.
bullets: Contributors uploaded real past projects: think a finance team's client-ready model, a creative agency's production asset, a lab's protocol review
An agent has to plan, execute, and produce the final artifact end-to-end inside the sandbox — not just answer a question about it
Grading checks the artifact itself against deterministic criteria, the same way a human reviewer would check deliverables

slides.title: What ALE Doesn't Tell You
bullets: It's a snapshot: VentureBeat reported GPT-5.5 already overtaking Claude Fable 5 head-to-head within weeks of release
Deterministic code grading is reproducible, but can penalize partially-correct or creatively-valid solutions a human might accept
The public corpus is 1,500 of a planned 5,000 tasks — today's ~26% baseline will keep moving as coverage grows

slides.title: Benchmark-Topping Isn't Job-Ready
bullets: An agent that scores 82% on Terminal-Bench can still fail 100% of the hardest real tasks in the same domain
The gap is in sustained, long-horizon execution — not single-step correctness
If you're deciding whether to trust an agent with real economic work, track ALE-style evals, not just leaderboard scores

questions.prompt: What does ALE primarily grade agents against?
options: Multiple-choice trivia questions
Real, economically valuable professional tasks with deterministic evaluators
Chatbot conversation quality
Pure math olympiad problems
questions.explanation: ALE draws tasks from 55 sub-industries mapped to real professional work, and grades the final artifact with deterministic code evaluators rather than human judges or multiple-choice format.

questions.prompt: On ALE's hardest 'last-exam' tier, how did frontier agents — including ones that score 82% on Terminal-Bench — perform?
options: Around 80%
Around 50%
Near 0%, averaging roughly 2.6%
A perfect 100%
questions.explanation: Despite topping existing benchmarks, frontier agents averaged only about 2.6% on ALE's hardest tier, with many configurations scoring a flat 0% — showing benchmark performance doesn't guarantee real-world task completion.

questions.prompt: Why might a model that dominates Terminal-Bench still collapse on ALE?
options: ALE uses easier tasks than Terminal-Bench
ALE only tests short, single-turn prompts
ALE demands sustained, long-horizon execution on unconstrained real-world tasks, which surfaces failure modes shorter benchmarks hide
ALE is graded subjectively by humans, introducing bias
questions.explanation: ALE tasks run in real OS sandboxes with no scaffolding across long horizons — exposing compounding errors and domain-expertise gaps that shorter, more constrained benchmarks don't reveal.

references.title: Agents' Last Exam
Agents' Last Exam — Berkeley RDI

posts.title: Inkling: Mira Murati's Lab Ships a 975B Open Model
posts.subtitle: Thinking Machines' first release is open-weight, multimodal, and lets you dial reasoning effort up or down.
posts.summary: Thinking Machines Lab, founded by ex-OpenAI CTO Mira Murati, released Inkling: a 975B-parameter, Apache 2.0 mixture-of-experts model with 41B active parameters, native multimodal training, and a controllable 'thinking effort' dial.

slides.title: The Largest US Open-Weight Model Yet
bullets: Released July 15, 2026 by Thinking Machines Lab (Mira Murati, ex-OpenAI CTO)
975B total parameters, 41B active per token — Apache 2.0, weights on Hugging Face
First public model from an 18-month-old lab betting against one-size-fits-all AI

slides.title: Why Ship Open Weights At All?
slides.body: Thinking Machines built its reputation on Tinker, a model fine-tuning platform — Inkling is the base customers customize, not a finished frontier chatbot.
bullets: Not positioned as the single strongest model, open or closed
Designed as a strong, customizable base: multimodal, efficient, tunable
Pitched around resisting over-centralization — anyone can download and fine-tune it directly

slides.title: Inside the 975B MoE
bullets: 66-layer decoder-only transformer with a sparse mixture-of-experts feed-forward backbone
256 experts per layer; each token routes to 6, plus 2 shared experts that fire on every token
Trained on 45 trillion tokens spanning text, image, audio, and video
1M-token context window; accepts text, image, and audio inputs

slides.title: Thinking Effort You Can Dial
bullets: A 'thinking effort' knob trades latency and cost for accuracy on a per-call basis
At effort 0.99: 97.1% on AIME 2026, 87.9% on GPQA Diamond, 77.6% on SWE-bench Verified
Built to flag uncertainty and give calibrated answers instead of guessing

slides.title: The Numbers
Who Actually Uses This
slides.body: The typical Inkling user isn't chatting with it directly — they're building on it.
bullets: A startup with a narrow, high-value task (e.g. legal document review) downloads the weights and fine-tunes via Tinker instead of prompting a closed frontier model
Because it's Apache 2.0, they can deploy the tuned checkpoint anywhere — no API dependency
Trade-off: they take on their own inference infrastructure for a 975B-parameter model

slides.title: Where Inkling Falls Short
bullets: Not the strongest model overall — closed frontier models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google still lead on raw capability
975B total parameters puts real self-hosting out of reach for most teams; most will use it via API or Tinker anyway
Being brand new, third-party tooling and quantized variants are still catching up

slides.title: Why It Matters
bullets: Inkling matters less as 'best model' and more as the first serious open-weight base with frontier-scale MoE plus native multimodality
The controllable thinking-effort dial is the most practically useful idea here — cost and latency become a per-call decision, not a per-model one
Watch Tinker adoption, not just raw benchmark scores, to see if this bet pays off

questions.prompt: What company released Inkling, and who founded it?
options: OpenAI, founded by Sam Altman
Thinking Machines Lab, founded by ex-OpenAI CTO Mira Murati
Google DeepMind, founded by Demis Hassabis
Mistral, founded by former Meta researchers
questions.explanation: Inkling is Thinking Machines Lab's first model release; the lab was founded by Mira Murati, OpenAI's former CTO.

questions.prompt: What is Inkling's architecture?
options: A dense 70B transformer
A diffusion-based image model
A 66-layer MoE transformer with 975B total / 41B active parameters
A retrieval-augmented small model
questions.explanation: Inkling is a sparse mixture-of-experts transformer: 66 layers, 256 experts per layer, routing each token to 6 experts plus 2 always-on shared experts, for 975B total and 41B active parameters.

questions.prompt: What does Inkling's 'thinking effort' control let users do?
options: Change the model's personality
Trade latency and cost for accuracy on a per-call basis
Switch between languages
Manually select which expert layer to use
questions.explanation: The thinking-effort dial lets a caller ask for more or less reasoning per request, making cost and latency tunable per call rather than fixed by which model you picked.

references.title: Inkling: Our open-weights model — Thinking Machines Lab
Welcome Inkling by Thinking Machines — Hugging Face

posts.title: FlashAttention: Exact Attention Without the Memory Blowup
posts.subtitle: Same math, radically less memory traffic — how tiling and online softmax made long context practical.
posts.summary: FlashAttention rewrites the attention computation as an IO-aware, fused GPU kernel: by tiling Q/K/V and computing softmax incrementally, it produces the exact same output as standard attention while avoiding the O(N²) memory traffic that made long context slow and memory-hungry.

slides.title: The Kernel That Made Long Context Affordable
bullets: Standard attention materializes an N×N score matrix — memory scales quadratically with sequence length
FlashAttention computes the exact same output while touching that matrix only in small tiles
Result: roughly 2-4x faster training and inference, no accuracy trade-off

slides.title: Why Vanilla Attention Chokes on Long Sequences
slides.body: The bottleneck isn't FLOPs — it's memory bandwidth, from repeatedly writing and reading the full attention matrix to slow GPU HBM.
bullets: For sequence length N, storing softmax(QK^T) costs O(N²) memory
At N=16K that's hundreds of MB per attention head just for one intermediate matrix
GPUs are compute-rich but bandwidth-poor: the real cost is shuttling that matrix between HBM and on-chip SRAM, not the matmuls themselves

slides.title: Tiling + Online Softmax
bullets: Split Q, K, V into blocks small enough to fit in fast on-chip SRAM
Compute attention per block pair, updating a running output and softmax normalizer incrementally ('online softmax')
The full N×N matrix is never materialized in slow HBM — only the final output and small per-row statistics get written back
Backward pass recomputes needed blocks on the fly instead of storing the full matrix — a little extra compute for a lot less memory traffic

slides.title: IO-Awareness Is the Whole Trick
slides.body: FlashAttention doesn't change what attention computes — it's mathematically exact, not an approximation like sparse or linear attention.
bullets: Kernel fusion: QK^T, softmax, and the value-weighted sum happen in one fused GPU kernel instead of separate ops with intermediate writes
FlashAttention-2 improved GPU occupancy and parallelized across sequence length, not just batch and heads
FlashAttention-3 (Hopper-era) exploits asynchronous execution and low-precision FP8 paths on newer GPUs for further speedups

slides.title: Exact, Not Approximate
What Changes in Practice
bullets: Fine-tuning a 7B model at 8K context: standard attention can exhaust GPU memory before you even fit a batch of 2
Swap in FlashAttention — default in most modern stacks (PyTorch SDPA, vLLM, Hugging Face Transformers) — and the same GPU handles the same context with room for a larger batch
No accuracy change: outputs are numerically the same attention computation, just computed with far less memory traffic

slides.title: When It's Not the Whole Answer
bullets: FlashAttention speeds up computing attention — it doesn't shrink the KV cache; for that you still need GQA/MLA or quantization
Gains are hardware-dependent: benefits are largest where the SRAM/HBM bandwidth gap is biggest (modern NVIDIA GPUs); kernels lag on new hardware until ported
Still O(N) memory for activations at inference — necessary for long context, but not sufficient on its own

slides.title: The Core Idea, Compressed
bullets: FlashAttention is exact attention rewritten as an IO-aware, fused GPU kernel — no math changes, just memory-traffic engineering
Tiling and online softmax avoid ever storing the full N×N attention matrix in slow memory
It's the default in virtually every serious training/inference stack today — pair it with GQA/MLA and quantization to actually shrink the KV cache too

questions.prompt: What is the main bottleneck FlashAttention targets?
options: The number of floating-point operations
Memory bandwidth — moving the attention matrix between GPU HBM and SRAM
Model parameter count
Network latency between GPUs
questions.explanation: GPUs have far more compute than memory bandwidth; the real cost of vanilla attention is repeatedly writing/reading the O(N²) score matrix to slow HBM, not the matmuls themselves.

questions.prompt: Is FlashAttention an approximation of standard attention?
options: Yes, it drops low-attention-score tokens
Yes, it uses a lower-precision softmax that changes results
No — it computes the mathematically exact same output, just with a different memory access pattern
No, but it only works for short sequences
questions.explanation: Unlike sparse or linear attention approximations, FlashAttention is exact — it produces identical outputs to standard attention by using tiling and online softmax to avoid unnecessary memory traffic.

questions.prompt: What does FlashAttention NOT solve on its own?
options: Slow training on long sequences
Quadratic memory for the intermediate attention matrix
Shrinking the KV cache size at inference time
Kernel fusion overhead
questions.explanation: FlashAttention makes computing attention memory-efficient, but the KV cache itself still grows with context length — techniques like GQA, MLA, or quantization are needed to shrink that separately.

references.title: FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness

posts.title: SAO: One Rollout Per Prompt, a Thousand Stable Steps
posts.subtitle: Tsinghua drops GRPO's groups to fix asynchronous agentic RL
posts.summary: SAO (Single-Rollout Asynchronous Optimization), from Zhenyu Hou, Yujiang Li, Jie Tang, and Yuxiao Dong at Tsinghua, replaces GRPO's group-wise sampling with one rollout per prompt so asynchronous agentic RL stops collapsing after a few hundred steps. In the paper's runs it trained stably past 1,000 steps and beat GRPO variants on SWE-Bench Verified, BeyondAIME, and IMOAnswerBench.

slides.title: Async RL for agents keeps collapsing — this paper asks why
bullets: arXiv 2607.07508, submitted July 8, 2026
Zhenyu Hou, Yujiang Li, Jie Tang, Yuxiao Dong — Tsinghua University
Jie Tang co-leads the GLM/Zhipu lineage of open LLMs

slides.title: GRPO's groups don't survive going asynchronous
slides.body: GRPO estimates advantage by sampling a group of responses per prompt and comparing each to the group average. That's fine when training is synchronous — but agentic RL rollouts (multi-step tool use, long coding sessions) run at wildly different speeds.
bullets: A group can't be scored until its slowest member finishes
In async training, the model keeps updating while that slow rollout is still running
By the time the group's advantage is computed, it's stale — off-policy — relative to the current weights
Vanilla GRPO in this paper's setup collapses after roughly 160 steps of this

slides.title: Drop the group. Score one rollout against a learned baseline instead
slides.body: SAO replaces group-relative advantage with single-rollout sampling — one rollout per prompt, scored against a trained value model instead of a group average.
bullets: No group to wait on → no straggler-induced staleness
A value model supplies the baseline that GRPO used to get from group averaging
A strict double-sided token-level clipping strategy bounds how far any single update can move the policy
Together these keep single-rollout updates from being too noisy to train on

slides.title: SAO vs. GRPO in the paper's evaluations
bullets: Numbers as reported by the authors across agentic coding and reasoning benchmarks

slides.title: Picture an agentic coding RL run
slides.body: 100 parallel agents are each working a SWE-Bench-style repo task. Most finish in 30 seconds; one gets stuck in a debug loop for 10 minutes.
bullets: Under GRPO: that agent's group can't be scored until it finishes — the trainer either stalls or scores a stale group
Under SAO: each agent's single rollout is scored the moment it finishes, independently, against the value model
The stuck agent's slow trajectory never blocks the other 99 from updating the policy immediately

slides.title: Where SAO gives something up
slides.body: Single-rollout sampling has no built-in variance reduction from comparing against siblings — that's exactly what the group average provided.
bullets: Requires training a reasonably accurate value model — extra compute and another moving part to get wrong
If your pipeline is already synchronous and cheap to batch, GRPO's group averaging is simpler and doesn't need this fix
The clipping strategy is what keeps the noisier single-rollout signal usable — remove it and stability likely degrades

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Group-wise sampling (GRPO) implicitly assumes synchronous, similarly-paced rollouts — agentic tasks break that assumption
SAO's fix: one rollout per prompt + a learned value baseline + strict token-level clipping
Result in the paper: stable training past 1,000 steps vs. ~160 for vanilla GRPO, with gains on SWE-Bench Verified, BeyondAIME, and AIME 2025
The core lesson generalizes: when you go asynchronous, audit every part of your algorithm that assumed synchrony

questions.prompt: Why does GRPO's group-wise sampling struggle in asynchronous agentic RL?
options: Groups require more GPU memory than SAO
A group's advantage can't be computed until its slowest rollout finishes, so by the time it's used the policy has already moved on, making the update off-policy
GRPO cannot be used with tool-calling agents at all
Group-wise sampling only works with reasoning models, not coding models
questions.explanation: The staleness comes from waiting on the group's slowest member — not from a hard capability limit of GRPO itself.

questions.prompt: What replaces the group average as the advantage baseline in SAO?
options: A fixed constant baseline of zero
The average reward across the entire training run so far
A separately trained value model
The reward from the previous training step only
questions.explanation: SAO scores a single rollout against a learned value model's prediction, since there's no group left to average against.

questions.prompt: In the paper's results, roughly how long did vanilla GRPO train stably before collapsing, versus SAO?
options: GRPO ~160 steps vs. SAO 1,000+ steps
Both collapsed at the same point, around 500 steps
GRPO never collapsed, but was slower
SAO collapsed sooner because single-rollout signals are noisier
questions.explanation: This stability gap — ~160 steps vs. 1,000+ — is the paper's headline result, driven by removing group-induced staleness and adding token-level clipping.

references.title: Single-Rollout Asynchronous Optimization for Agentic Reinforcement Learning (arXiv 2607.07508)

posts.title: NVIDIA Cosmos 3: One Model Family for Physical AI
posts.subtitle: A two-tower world model that reasons about physics and generates it
posts.summary: NVIDIA's Cosmos 3 unifies vision-language reasoning and video/action generation into one omnimodal world-model family (Nano at 16B, Super at 64B, plus specialized variants), aimed at robotics, autonomous vehicles, and smart infrastructure. It ships open under the OpenMDW-1.1 license with BF16/FP8/NVFP4 support.

slides.title: NVIDIA Cosmos 3: reasoning and generation in one world model
bullets: Open platform for Physical AI: robots, autonomous vehicles, smart infrastructure
Announced via NVIDIA's official Cosmos research program and open-sourced on GitHub
Coincides with NVIDIA's physical-AI push in Japan alongside Fanuc and Yaskawa Electric

slides.title: Robots need to predict physics, not just describe it
slides.body: Before Cosmos 3, understanding a scene (vision-language models), generating video of what happens next (video generators), and choosing a physical action (robot policies) were three separate model families with no shared representation.
bullets: A VLM can describe a scene but can't simulate what happens if the robot acts
A video generator can imagine plausible footage but doesn't ground it in an actionable policy
Stitching these together loses information at every handoff

slides.title: A two-tower Mixture-of-Transformers
slides.body: Cosmos 3 pairs an autoregressive 'Reasoner' with a diffusion-based 'Generator' inside one omnimodal architecture, sharing a 3D rotary position embedding across space and time.
bullets: Reasoner mode: text + vision in, text out — captions, spatial grounding, physical plausibility, causal outcomes
Generator mode: text/image/video/action in, image/video/audio/action out — simulates what happens next
One shared backbone means the same model that judges 'is this grasp physically plausible' can also generate the rollout to check it

slides.title: The Cosmos 3 family
A warehouse robot deciding whether to trust a grasp
slides.body: A picking robot sees a box on a shelf and is told to retrieve it.
bullets: Reasoner mode interprets the camera feed plus the instruction, and flags whether a candidate grasp trajectory is physically plausible before any motor moves
Generator mode can roll forward a short simulated video of that grasp attempt, at up to 300 frames, to preview outcomes
The robot commits to the plan Cosmos 3 rates as most physically consistent — rather than executing open-loop and finding out it fails

slides.title: Where it breaks down
slides.body: NVIDIA's own documentation is explicit about the model's limits.
bullets: Long, high-resolution, or physically complex outputs can show temporal inconsistency, unstable motion, and implausible physics
Default generation is 480p at 24 FPS — pushing to 720p or long sequences is where artifacts increase
Requires Ampere/Hopper/Blackwell-class GPUs; this is not a lightweight edge model in its Nano/Super form
It's a world model, not a validated safety-certified controller — real deployments still need task-specific verification

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Cosmos 3 merges 'understand the scene' and 'simulate what happens next' into one two-tower architecture instead of three separate model families
Five variants span 16B–64B, covering reasoning, image/video generation, and robot policy
Open-sourced under OpenMDW-1.1 with BF16/FP8/NVFP4 quantization support
Ships alongside a real industrial push — NVIDIA paired the release with physical-AI partnerships in Japan (Fanuc, Yaskawa)

It unifies scene understanding (reasoning) and physical simulation (generation) in one shared representation, instead of stitching separate models together
It replaces the need for any robot hardware
It only works for text-based tasks, not vision
questions.explanation: The core pitch is a shared backbone: the same model that judges physical plausibility can also generate a rollout to test it, avoiding information loss between separate systems.

Cosmos3-Nano-Policy-DROID

Cosmos3-Super

options: Short, low-resolution clips
Text-only reasoning tasks

posts.title: Speculative Decoding: Guessing Ahead to Speed Up Inference
posts.subtitle: A small model drafts, the big model verifies — for free accuracy
posts.summary: Speculative decoding uses a small, fast draft model to guess several tokens ahead, then lets the large target model verify them all in a single forward pass — accepting correct guesses and only falling back to normal decoding on a mismatch. It speeds up generation 2-3x with mathematically identical output quality, but the gain depends entirely on how often the draft model guesses right.

slides.title: Why does the biggest GPU in the building still feel slow at chat?
bullets: Autoregressive decoding generates one token at a time, each needing a full model forward pass
At small batch sizes, that pass is memory-bandwidth bound — most of the GPU sits idle waiting to load weights
Speculative decoding exploits that idle capacity instead of buying more of it

slides.title: One token per pass wastes the GPU
slides.body: Generating token N+1 requires reading the entire model's weights from memory, even though the actual matrix multiply is cheap by comparison for a single token.
bullets: A100/H100-class GPUs can do far more compute per byte loaded than single-token decoding uses
If you could verify multiple tokens per weight-load, you'd amortize that cost across all of them
That's exactly what a parallel forward pass over several candidate tokens lets you do

slides.title: Draft small, verify big, in parallel
slides.body: A cheap draft model proposes K tokens one at a time (fast, since it's small). The large target model then runs a single forward pass over all K draft tokens at once — checking them in parallel instead of generating them one at a time.
bullets: Draft model: e.g. a 4B model guessing ahead for a 70B target model
Target model verifies all K guesses in one pass — same cost as generating one token normally, but checking several
Accepted tokens are free speedup; a rejection just falls back to standard next-token generation from that point

slides.title: The math that keeps output identical: rejection sampling
slides.body: For each drafted token, compare the draft model's probability q(x) against the target model's probability p(x) for that same token.
bullets: Accept the draft token with probability min(1, p(x)/q(x))
If rejected, resample from a corrected residual distribution — not just the target's raw distribution
This guarantees the final output distribution is exactly what the target model alone would have produced — no quality tradeoff, only speed

slides.title: Real measured speedups
bullets: Both report identical output distributions to standard decoding — the speedup is free, not a quality tradeoff

slides.title: Walking through one step
slides.body: Target model is generating code and the draft model proposes: 'for i in range(10):'
bullets: Target model checks all 6 drafted tokens in one parallel pass
Tokens 'for i in range(' match what the target would have produced with high probability — accepted
Token '10' diverges from what the target model would pick (maybe it wanted 'n') — rejected, resampled correctly from that point
Net result: 4 tokens accepted for the price of 1 target forward pass, then generation continues normally

slides.title: When it doesn't help — or actively hurts
slides.body: The entire speedup depends on the draft model guessing tokens the target model would also have picked.
bullets: Low acceptance rate (e.g. open-ended creative writing, where many tokens are equally plausible) means most draft tokens get rejected, wasting the draft model's work
Draft and target need matching tokenizers and reasonably similar output distributions, or acceptance rate craters
At large batch sizes, the target model is already compute-bound, not memory-bound — the idle capacity speculative decoding exploits shrinks or disappears
You now maintain and serve two models instead of one, with extra GPU memory for the draft model

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Speculative decoding turns idle memory-bandwidth-bound GPU capacity into throughput by verifying several draft tokens per forward pass
Rejection sampling guarantees the output distribution exactly matches standard decoding — this is a speed win with no quality tradeoff
Published results: 2-3x on T5-XXL, 2-2.5x on Chinchilla 70B with a 4B draft model
It shines on structured, low-entropy generation (code, deterministic answers) and helps least on high-entropy creative text or at high batch sizes

questions.prompt: Why is single-token autoregressive decoding often inefficient on a GPU?
options: GPUs cannot perform matrix multiplication efficiently
At small batch sizes it's memory-bandwidth bound — loading the model's weights dominates the cost, leaving compute capacity idle
It requires too much disk storage
Language models cannot be run on GPUs at all
questions.explanation: The weights must be streamed from memory for every single token generated, and that transfer cost — not the compute — is usually the bottleneck at low batch sizes.

questions.prompt: How does speculative decoding guarantee output quality doesn't degrade compared to standard decoding?
options: It doesn't guarantee this — it's a lossy approximation
The draft model's guesses are always trusted without verification
Rejection sampling accepts each draft token with probability min(1, p(x)/q(x)) and resamples correctly on rejection, making the final distribution identical to the target model alone
It only works if the draft and target models are the same size
questions.explanation: The rejection-sampling accept/resample rule is specifically designed so the output distribution matches what the target model would have produced on its own.

questions.prompt: In which scenario does speculative decoding provide the LEAST benefit?
options: Generating boilerplate code with a highly predictable structure
Open-ended creative writing at large batch sizes, where many tokens are equally plausible and the target model is already compute-bound
Answering deterministic factual questions
Any task using a well-aligned small draft model
questions.explanation: Low acceptance rates (high-entropy text) waste the draft model's guesses, and large batch sizes remove the idle memory-bandwidth capacity the technique relies on.

references.title: Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding (Leviathan et al., arXiv 2211.17192)
Accelerating Large Language Model Decoding with Speculative Sampling (Chen et al., arXiv 2302.01318)

posts.title: Grok 4.5: xAI Bets on Cursor-Trained Code
posts.subtitle: A 500K-context coding model priced to undercut the frontier.
posts.summary: xAI shipped Grok 4.5 on July 8, 2026 — a 500K-context model trained alongside Cursor and aimed squarely at coding and agentic work. It undercuts frontier pricing at $2/$6 per million tokens while Musk pitches it as 'Opus 4.7-class, but faster and cheaper.'

slides.title: xAI's Answer to the Coding-Agent Price War
slides.body: On July 8, 2026, xAI introduced Grok 4.5 — its most capable model yet, built specifically for coding, agentic tasks, and knowledge work.

slides.title: Frontier Coding Models Are Expensive and Crowded
slides.body: By mid-2026 every major lab has an agentic coding model, and the differentiator has shifted from raw capability to cost-per-token and tool-use reliability.
bullets: GPT-5.6, Claude Opus 4.8/Sonnet 5, and Gemini already compete for the same coding-agent workloads
Long agentic sessions burn tokens fast — price per million tokens matters as much as benchmark scores
xAI needed a model developers would route through their IDE daily, not just try once as a demo

slides.title: Trained Alongside Cursor, Priced to Undercut
slides.body: Grok 4.5 was trained in partnership with Cursor, xAI's coding-tool collaborator, on data spanning coding, science, engineering, and math.
bullets: 500K-token context window — enough to hold a mid-size repo's worth of files in one session
Served at roughly 80 tokens/second
$2 per million input tokens / $6 per million output tokens
Available same-day inside Grok Build, in Cursor on all plans, and via the xAI API console

slides.title: Musk's Pitch: Opus-Class Speed at a Discount
slides.body: xAI positioned Grok 4.5 directly against Anthropic's Opus line rather than against GPT-5.6.

rows: "Roughly comparable to Opus 4.7, but much faster" and cheaper

slides.title: A Multi-File Refactor, End to End
slides.body: Picture a developer inside Cursor asking Grok 4.5 to rename a config schema across a 40-file service.
bullets: The 500K context window lets it ingest most of the relevant repo without manual file selection
At ~80 tok/s, a multi-step edit-test-fix loop finishes in roughly the time of a coffee break
Priced at $2/$6 per million tokens, the same agentic loop costs a fraction of premium frontier pricing

slides.title: Not Available Everywhere, Not Free of Rough Edges
slides.body: The launch came with real caveats worth knowing before routing production workloads through it.
bullets: Not available in the EU in any xAI product or the API console at launch — EU access was expected only in mid-July
Early third-party coverage flagged higher hallucination rates than some top frontier models even as raw agentic speed impressed
Independent benchmark rankings vary by suite and month — treat any single leaderboard number as a snapshot, not a verdict

bullets: Grok 4.5 launched July 8, 2026 as xAI's coding- and agent-focused flagship, trained alongside Cursor
500K context, ~80 tok/s, $2/$6 per million tokens — priced as a cheaper, faster alternative to Opus-class models
Ships in Grok Build, Cursor, and the xAI API — but not yet in the EU
Cheaper and faster isn't the same as more reliable — weigh hallucination reports before high-stakes use

posts.title: PalmClaw: Mobile Agents That Call Tools, Not Taps
posts.subtitle: An on-device framework that ditches screen-tapping for real device APIs.
posts.summary: PalmClaw (arXiv 2607.13027) is a native on-device agent framework that replaces GUI tapping and swiping with direct tool calls to phone capabilities. On the new MobileTask benchmark it beats the strongest baseline by 11.5% relative task success while cutting completion time by 94.9%.

slides.title: Teaching Phone Agents to Stop Tapping Screens
slides.body: Most mobile AI agents today still operate a phone the way a human would — tapping, swiping, typing through app UIs. PalmClaw asks: what if the agent just called the underlying functions directly?

slides.title: GUI-Only Agents Are Slow and Fragile by Design
slides.body: Existing mobile agents mainly control phones through GUI actions.
bullets: Tapping/swiping/typing produces long, interface-dependent action sequences — one UI redesign can break the whole chain
GUI agents can't directly reach device capabilities that aren't exposed as visible screens
Execution boundaries (when is a 'task' actually done?) are hard to define from pixels alone

slides.title: An Agent Loop That Lives on the Device
slides.body: PalmClaw is an open-source framework that runs natively on the phone, managing sessions, memory, skills, and tools directly on-device instead of routing everything through screen coordinates.
bullets: Structured tool calls replace tap/swipe sequences wherever a device capability can be exposed as an API
Skills and memory persist locally between tasks instead of being rebuilt from scratch each session
The agent loop (plan → call tool → observe → continue) runs on-device, cutting round-trips

slides.title: MobileTask: The Numbers
slides.body: MobileTask is a new 70-task benchmark adapted from AndroidWorld and MobileAgentBench, built to test real mobile workflows.

slides.title: Booking a Table for Four at 7PM
slides.body: A concrete task shows the difference in kind, not just degree.
bullets: A GUI agent opens the reservations app, taps through a date picker, a time picker, a party-size field, then a confirm screen — 15-20+ actions, each a chance to mistap or hit a redesigned layout
PalmClaw instead calls a reservation-capable tool directly with structured parameters (party_size=4, time=19:00), needing far fewer steps
With no screenshots to render and re-parse between steps, the task finishes in a fraction of the wall-clock time — consistent with the paper's 94.9% completion-time reduction

slides.title: It Only Works Where There's a Tool to Call
slides.body: Native tool-calling isn't a free upgrade — it depends on what the device and its apps actually expose.
bullets: Apps that don't expose structured APIs still need a GUI fallback, so PalmClaw can't fully replace screen-based agents yet
Building and maintaining a tool/skill library per capability is more engineering upfront than 'just let the agent tap around'
On-device execution shifts compute and battery cost onto the phone itself rather than a server

slides.title: What to Remember
bullets: PalmClaw runs the full agent loop — memory, skills, tools — natively on the phone instead of orchestrating GUI taps
On MobileTask (70 tasks from AndroidWorld + MobileAgentBench), it beats the strongest baseline by 11.5% relative task success and 94.9% less completion time
It also tops AssistantBench accuracy among compared agents
The core idea generalizes beyond phones: wherever an agent can call a real API instead of faking a UI interaction, expect it to be faster and more reliable

questions.prompt: What does PalmClaw replace GUI tapping/swiping with?
options: Voice commands only
Direct structured tool calls to device capabilities
A faster screenshot pipeline
Cloud-only execution
questions.explanation: PalmClaw's core idea is calling device capabilities as structured tools directly, instead of simulating human taps and swipes through the UI.

questions.prompt: What benchmark did PalmClaw's authors introduce to evaluate it?
options: AndroidWorld
MobileTask
SWE-bench
AssistantBench
questions.explanation: MobileTask is a 70-task benchmark adapted from AndroidWorld and MobileAgentBench, purpose-built to evaluate real mobile agent workflows.

questions.prompt: What is the main limitation of PalmClaw's approach?
options: It requires a data-center GPU
It only works when a device capability is exposed as a callable tool
It cannot use any tools at all
It only works on a single phone model
questions.explanation: Native tool-calling depends on the capability actually being exposed as an API — apps without one still need a GUI-based fallback.

references.title: PalmClaw: A Native On-Device Agent Framework for Mobile Phones (arXiv:2607.13027)

posts.title: Consistent Hashing: Scaling Caches Without a Reshuffle
posts.subtitle: Why adding one server shouldn't invalidate everyone else's cache.
posts.summary: Naive hash(key) % N sharding remaps almost every key when a cache cluster resizes, causing a stampede of misses. Consistent hashing places keys and servers on the same ring so only a neighbor's share of keys move — and virtual nodes make the resulting load balance even.

slides.title: The Hash Ring That Saved Distributed Caching
slides.body: Add or remove one server from a naive hash-based cache cluster, and almost every key remaps at once. Consistent hashing fixes that with one idea: a ring.

slides.title: Mod-N Hashing Breaks Almost Everything on Resize
slides.body: The obvious way to shard N cache servers is key -> hash(key) % N. It works — until N changes.
bullets: Go from 4 servers to 5, and hash(key) % 4 vs hash(key) % 5 disagree for almost every key
Roughly (N-1)/N of all keys remap to a different server on a single node change — with 4 servers, that's ~75-80% of your cache
Every remapped key is a guaranteed cache miss, all landing on your backend at once — a self-inflicted stampede exactly when the cluster is already changing

slides.title: Hash Both Keys and Servers Onto the Same Ring
slides.body: Consistent hashing maps both cache keys and server IDs into the same circular hash space (e.g., 0 to 2^32-1).
bullets: Each key is owned by the first server found walking clockwise from the key's position on the ring
Adding a server only steals keys from its immediate clockwise neighbor — every other server's keys stay put
Removing a server only reassigns its keys to its clockwise neighbor — again, everyone else is untouched

slides.title: Virtual Nodes Fix the Uneven-Load Problem
slides.body: A bare ring with one point per server can distribute load very unevenly — one server might get a huge, unlucky arc.
bullets: Give each physical server 100-200 'virtual node' points scattered around the ring instead of just one
Load balances much closer to 1/N per server on average, since bad luck on one virtual point is smoothed out by the others
This is exactly how systems like Cassandra and Amazon DynamoDB spread partitions across physical nodes

slides.title: Adding a 5th Node to a 4-Node Cache
slides.body: Concretely: with plain mod-N hashing, moving from 4 to 5 servers remaps roughly 80% of keys. With consistent hashing, the new node only claims keys in the ring arc between itself and its clockwise neighbor.
bullets: If the ring is evenly divided, a 5th node claims roughly 1/5 (~20%) of the keys, not ~80%
The other 4 servers keep every key they already owned — zero disruption to their portion of the cache
This is why you can autoscale a cache cluster under load without a mass cache-miss stampede

slides.title: Mod-N vs. Consistent Hashing vs. Consistent Hashing + Vnodes

rows: Even, but only until N changes

slides.title: It's Not a Free Lunch
slides.body: Consistent hashing solves resharding pain, not every distributed-cache problem.
bullets: It doesn't handle replication by itself — you still need a separate policy (e.g., 'store on the next R distinct nodes clockwise') for durability
More moving parts than mod-N: you maintain a ring structure and virtual-node mapping instead of one modulo operation
Skip it for small, static clusters that never resize — the added complexity buys you nothing if N never changes
A ring rebuild during heavy churn (many nodes joining/leaving at once) can still cause a burst of cache misses

slides.title: What to Remember
bullets: Naive hash % N remaps almost all keys on every resize — a self-inflicted cache stampede
Consistent hashing puts keys and servers on the same ring so only a neighbor's worth of keys move on resize
Virtual nodes (100-200 per server) are what make the load balance actually even in practice
Used in Cassandra, DynamoDB, and countless memcached/Redis cluster implementations — reach for it whenever nodes join or leave a sharded store

questions.prompt: With plain hash(key) % N sharding, what happens when you go from 4 to 5 servers?
options: Nothing remaps, since the servers are still up
Roughly 75-80% of keys remap to a different server
Only the new server's share of keys remap
Exactly 20% of keys remap
questions.explanation: Because % 4 and % 5 disagree for almost every key, adding one server to a 4-node mod-N cluster remaps roughly (N-1)/N ≈ 75-80% of all keys at once.

questions.prompt: In consistent hashing, which server owns a given key?
options: The server with the numerically closest ID
The first server found walking clockwise from the key's position on the ring
A randomly chosen server each time
Whichever server has the most free memory
questions.explanation: Consistent hashing places both keys and servers on a ring; a key belongs to the next server encountered walking clockwise from the key's own hash position.

questions.prompt: Why do real systems add virtual nodes on top of consistent hashing?
options: To make the hash function faster
To smooth out uneven load that a single point per server can produce
To enable encryption of cache keys
To reduce the total number of servers needed
questions.explanation: A single ring point per server can leave some servers with a much larger arc (and thus more keys) than others by bad luck; giving each server 100-200 virtual points averages this out closer to 1/N per node.

posts.title: Ring-Zero: Scaling Zero RL to a Trillion Parameters
posts.subtitle: Ant Group's InclusionAI team takes reward-free RL reasoning training out of the small-model lab and into a 1T-parameter MoE — and finds new failure modes waiting at scale.
posts.summary: Ring-Zero is InclusionAI's (Ant Group) study of 'zero RL' — reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards but no human-annotated data — scaled to a trillion-parameter MoE for the first time. Stabilizing that scale required new training-pipeline fixes, and the resulting model spontaneously developed reasoning behaviors like self-verification and 'context anxiety' that were never explicitly trained for.

slides.title: What Happens When Zero RL Meets a Trillion Parameters?
slides.body: Zero RL — training a base model to reason with verifiable rewards (right/wrong answers) instead of human-labeled preference data — has mostly been studied on small models. InclusionAI, Ant Group's open-model research team behind the Ring/Ling family, ran the recipe at 1T-parameter scale to see what changes.

slides.title: Small-Model Recipes Don't Just 'Scale Up'
bullets: Zero RL skips a learned reward model entirely — it rewards a rollout only if its final answer is automatically verifiable (a math result, a passing test), which makes it cheap and label-free.

Almost all prior zero RL work stopped at small or mid-size models because of compute cost, so nobody knew whether the training dynamics — or the reasoning behaviors that emerge — hold up at trillion-parameter scale.

Naively scaling the same recipe up introduces new failure modes: poor readability, token redundancy (rambling, repetitive chains), and reasoning depth that doesn't adapt to how hard the question actually is.

slides.title: Three Fixes to Keep Trillion-Scale RL Stable
bullets: Clipped importance sampling bounds how much any single rollout's probability ratio can swing the policy update — without it, a handful of outlier rollouts can destabilize a model this large.

Training-inference ratio correction reconciles small numerical mismatches between the policy used to generate rollouts and the one being updated — a gap that's negligible at small scale but compounds at 1T parameters.
Mixed-precision control manages where the pipeline can safely drop precision for speed versus where it must stay high-precision to avoid gradient corruption over long RL runs.

slides.title: Reasoning Behaviors the Model Was Never Told to Learn
bullets: Training progresses through two phases: a 'discovery' phase where the model explores varied reasoning strategies, then a 'sharpening' phase where it converges on and refines the ones that work.

Without any hand-crafted heuristics for it, the model spontaneously developed behaviors researchers grouped under labels like self-verification (checking its own intermediate steps), parallel reasoning (exploring branches at once), structured formatting, anthropomorphism, and 'context anxiety.'

'Context anxiety' describes the model changing its reasoning behavior as it senses it's approaching its context limit — e.g. compressing or rushing toward an answer — a side effect nobody explicitly trained for.

slides.title: What Breaks at Scale vs. What Ring-Zero Fixes

rows: Outlier rollouts destabilize the policy update
Generation vs. update numerical mismatch compounds
Long RL runs corrupt gradients under low precision
Naive scaling produces rambling, redundant chains

slides.title: Worked Example: Why 'Just Add More Compute' Fails Here
bullets: Take the same zero RL recipe that works cleanly on a 7B model and run it unmodified on a 1T-parameter MoE: without ratio correction, tiny train/inference numerical drift — invisible at 7B — accumulates over thousands of steps into policy updates that actively point the wrong direction.
The result before Ring-Zero's fixes: training reasoning traces get longer without getting better, a symptom of token redundancy rather than deeper reasoning.

After the fixes, the same compute budget produces reasoning that the paper's own evaluation judged along three axes — comprehensibility, reproducibility, and efficiency — not just raw accuracy, because a correct-but-unreadable chain is still a practical failure.

slides.title: Where This Doesn't Help You
bullets: This is a training-time recipe for organizations that can pretrain or RL-tune a trillion-parameter MoE — it's not something you replicate on a fine-tuning budget; the fixes exist specifically because the failure modes only appear at that scale.

The 'emergent behaviors' are observed and named, not fully explained — the paper reports that they show up, not a mechanistic account of why, so treat labels like 'context anxiety' as descriptive, not causal.
Because reward comes only from automatically verifiable answers, zero RL is a poor fit for open-ended tasks (writing, subjective judgment) where 'correct' isn't a checkable label in the first place.

slides.title: Scale Doesn't Just Amplify — It Introduces New Bugs
bullets: Zero RL's training dynamics genuinely change at trillion-parameter scale: numerical mismatches and outlier updates that were noise at small scale become the dominant failure mode.
Clipped importance sampling, training-inference ratio correction, and mixed-precision control are the specific levers InclusionAI used to keep training stable at 1T parameters.

Emergent reasoning behaviors (self-verification, parallel reasoning, context anxiety) showing up unprompted is a reminder that scaling RL, not just pretraining, can surface capabilities nobody explicitly designed for — for better and for worse.

questions.prompt: What distinguishes 'zero RL' from standard RLHF-style training?
options: Zero RL rewards rollouts using automatically verifiable answers instead of a learned reward model trained on human preference labels
Zero RL uses no reinforcement learning at all
Zero RL requires zero compute compared to RLHF
Zero RL only works on models under 10B parameters
questions.explanation: Zero RL skips a human-labeled reward model entirely, scoring rollouts only when the final answer can be automatically checked (like a math result), which is what makes it cheap but also limits it to verifiable-answer domains.

questions.prompt: Why does training-inference ratio correction matter more at 1T parameters than at 7B?
options: It doesn't — the correction is only cosmetic
Small numerical mismatches between the rollout-generating policy and the policy being updated are negligible at small scale but compound over thousands of steps at trillion-parameter scale
Larger models don't use ratios at all
It only affects inference speed, not training
questions.explanation: The gap between the policy used to generate rollouts and the one being updated is present at any scale, but at 7B it's noise; at 1T parameters, uncorrected, it accumulates into policy updates that point in the wrong direction.

questions.prompt: What does 'context anxiety,' one of the paper's observed emergent behaviors, describe?
options: The model refusing to answer questions about its own training
A hand-crafted heuristic the researchers explicitly programmed
The model changing its reasoning behavior — e.g. rushing or compressing — as it senses it's approaching its context limit, without being explicitly trained to do so
A bug that only appears during inference, never during training
questions.explanation: Context anxiety was one of several reasoning behaviors that emerged spontaneously during RL training rather than being hand-designed — the model appears to adapt its reasoning style as it nears its context window's limit.

references.title: Ring-Zero: Scaling Zero RL to a Trillion Parameters for Emergent Reasoning (arXiv:2607.12395)
