posts.title: Claude for Teachers: Free Claude for Every US K-12 Educator
posts.subtitle: Anthropic ties Claude to state academic standards and trusted curricula, betting that classrooms need standards-aligned AI, not just a chatbot.
posts.summary: On July 14, 2026, Anthropic launched Claude for Teachers, giving verified US K-12 educators free access to premium Claude features connected to state academic standards and trusted curricula like OpenSciEd. The release bundles nine education-tool connectors, a FERPA-compliant data agreement, and a co-developed AI literacy course for teachers.

slides.title: Anthropic Bets Classrooms Need Standards-Aware AI
slides.body: Claude for Teachers gives verified K-12 educators across all 50 US states free access to premium Claude capabilities, plus a direct connection to academic standards and vetted curricula — not just a general-purpose chatbot.

slides.title: A Generic Chatbot Doesn't Know Your State's Standards
bullets: A teacher asking any general LLM for a lesson plan gets something plausible-sounding but disconnected from the specific academic standard, grade-level competency, or district-approved curriculum they're required to teach to.
Schools also need real privacy guarantees: student data touching a chatbot has to satisfy FERPA, and 'we promise not to misuse it' isn't a substitute for a signed data processing agreement.

Teachers already use a patchwork of edtech tools (problem generators, activity builders, presentation tools) — a lesson-planning assistant that doesn't connect to any of them just adds another disconnected step.

slides.title: Learning Commons: Standards Wired Directly Into Claude
bullets: Claude for Teachers connects to 'Learning Commons,' giving Claude access to academic standards across all 50 states plus the smaller sub-competencies beneath each standard.
That means a generated lesson plan is scaffolded and explicitly aligned to the actual standard a teacher is required to teach, not a generic approximation of grade-level content.
The release also bakes in trusted curricular resources — OpenSciEd for science, and IM v.360 from Illustrative Mathematics for math — so outputs draw from materials schools already vet and trust.

slides.title: Nine Connectors Into the Tools Teachers Already Use
bullets: Claude for Teachers ships with nine education connectors rather than working in isolation — including ASSISTments for generating auto-scored, standards-aligned math problems.
Brisk Teaching lets Claude produce interactive student activities and standards-aligned lessons directly usable in class.

Canva Education turns lesson materials Claude drafts into classroom-ready designs and interactive learning experiences — closing the gap between 'AI-generated text' and 'something you can actually hand a student.'

slides.title: Worked Example: Planning a Standards-Aligned Lesson
bullets: A 7th-grade math teacher asks Claude to build a lesson on solving linear equations for their state's specific standard.
Claude for Teachers pulls the exact sub-competency from Learning Commons, drafts a plan scaffolded to it, and pulls in an IM v.360-aligned activity instead of inventing one from scratch.

Through the ASSISTments and Canva Education connectors, that plan becomes an auto-scored practice set and a classroom-ready printable handout — without the teacher manually rebuilding the output in three different tools.

slides.title: Generic Assistant vs. Claude for Teachers
The Fine Print and the Risk Anthropic Itself Flags
bullets: Access is free only for verified US K-12 educators — not higher-ed instructors, not international teachers, and eligibility must be verified, so it's not a universal free tier.
Teachers who sign up by June 30, 2027 lock in a full year of access — after that date the terms of continued access aren't guaranteed to be the same.

Anthropic co-created an 'AI Fluency for PK-12 Teachers' course with Teach For America specifically to teach where AI is and isn't appropriate in a classroom — a tacit admission that handing teachers a capable model without guidance is itself a risk worth addressing.

slides.title: Standards Alignment, Not Raw Capability, Is the Pitch
bullets: Claude for Teachers' actual differentiator isn't a smarter model — it's Learning Commons wiring Claude into state-specific standards and vetted curricula that a generic chatbot has no access to.
Nine connectors (ASSISTments, Brisk Teaching, Canva Education, and others) turn Claude's output into classroom-ready artifacts instead of text a teacher still has to reformat by hand.
The FERPA-aligned data agreement and no-training-on-student-data commitment are the actual unlock for school adoption — capability alone doesn't clear a district's privacy bar.

questions.prompt: What does the 'Learning Commons' connector give Claude for Teachers access to?
options: A general web search index
Academic standards and sub-competencies across all 50 US states
A database of student grades
A generic library of stock lesson plans with no standards mapping
questions.explanation: Learning Commons is what lets Claude scaffold a lesson plan to the exact standard and sub-competency a teacher must teach to, rather than a generic approximation.

questions.prompt: What data commitment does Anthropic make for Claude for Teachers?
options: Student data is used to fine-tune future Claude models
Student data is not used for model training, covered by a FERPA-aligned K-12 data processing addendum
No data commitments are made beyond the standard consumer terms
Data is deleted immediately and no logs are kept for any purpose
questions.explanation: The release explicitly states student data isn't used for model training and is covered by a K-12 data processing addendum written to comply with FERPA — a specific compliance guarantee schools require.

questions.prompt: What is the deadline for teachers to sign up and lock in a full year of free access?
options: December 31, 2026
There is no deadline
The end of the current school semester
questions.explanation: Teachers who sign up for Claude for Teachers by June 30, 2027 receive a full year of access — a concrete cutoff, not an open-ended free tier.

posts.title: Context Engineering: Curating What the Model Actually Sees
posts.subtitle: Long-running agents rarely fail from a bad prompt — they fail from a cluttered context window. Anthropic's playbook: compact it, take notes outside it, or delegate the mess to a subagent.
posts.summary: Context engineering reframes agent design around a scarce resource: the context window's finite attention budget. Rather than maximizing what you stuff into a prompt, the goal is the smallest set of high-signal tokens that reliably produces the desired behavior — using techniques like compaction, structured note-taking, and multi-agent delegation to keep long-running agents from drowning in their own history.

slides.title: Prompting Was the Easy Part — Context Is the Hard Part
slides.body: As agents run longer — dozens of tool calls, hundreds of turns — the bottleneck stops being 'what words go in the prompt' and becomes 'what configuration of context is most likely to produce the model's desired behavior.' That's context engineering.

slides.title: A Context Window Is a Finite, Degrading Resource
bullets: An LLM's attention is a finite budget spread across every token in the window — adding more tokens doesn't add more signal, and past a point it adds noise ('context pollution').
Long-running agents accumulate tool outputs, dead-end retries, and stale intermediate plans across many turns; that irrelevant history competes with the current task for the model's attention.
The naive fix — just use a bigger context window — doesn't solve this: the problem is signal density, not raw capacity, so a bigger window fills with the same proportion of noise.

slides.title: Compaction: Summarize, Then Restart
bullets: When a conversation nears the context limit, compaction summarizes everything so far and reinitializes a fresh context window seeded with that summary instead of the full raw history.
The tuning order matters: first maximize recall so the compaction prompt captures every relevant fact from the trace, then iterate to improve precision by trimming anything superfluous.
Get that order backwards — optimize for precision first — and the summary can quietly drop a detail the agent turns out to need several turns later, with no obvious signal that anything went wrong.

slides.title: Structured Note-Taking: Memory Outside the Window
bullets: Instead of keeping everything live in context, the agent periodically writes structured notes — a running todo list, key facts, decisions made — to persistent memory outside the context window.
This is 'agentic memory': the agent can let the raw conversation age out of context and still recover what mattered by reading its own notes back in when needed.
It trades a small per-step write/read overhead for a context window that stays close to the size of the actual current task, rather than the size of everything that's happened so far.

slides.title: Multi-Agent Delegation: Keep the Mess Off the Main Thread
bullets: A subagent gets its own fresh context window to do one bounded piece of work — search a codebase, read a document, run an exploratory query — and returns only a distilled result to the parent.
The orchestrating agent's main context never sees the subagent's raw exploration, tool call retries, or dead ends — only the summary it needed.
The tradeoff: the parent loses visibility into the subagent's raw reasoning trace, so this only pays off when the subagent's returned summary genuinely captures what the parent actually needs.

slides.title: Worked Example: A 40-Turn Debugging Agent
bullets: An agent debugging a failing test runs 40 tool calls — reading files, running the suite, checking logs. By turn 30, most of that raw output is dead weight.
Without context engineering: the full 40-turn transcript is fed back in every step, diluting the model's attention right when it most needs to reason precisely about the actual remaining bug.

With compaction plus structured notes: at turn 20 the agent compacts to 'tried X (failed — wrong import), tried Y (partial fix, one test still failing)' and keeps a running notes file — turn 40's context is a fraction of the size and almost entirely signal.

slides.title: When Context Engineering Isn't Worth It
bullets: Short, single-turn tasks don't need compaction or note-taking machinery — the overhead of summarizing and re-reading notes can cost more than the pollution it would have prevented.

Compaction is lossy by construction: tuned precision-first instead of recall-first, it can silently drop a fact the agent needs later, and that failure is invisible until the agent acts on incomplete information.
Multi-agent delegation adds real latency and coordination overhead — it only pays off when the delegated task is genuinely boundable and can be compressed into a small, faithful summary.

slides.title: The Skill Is Curation, Not Just Prompting
bullets: Context engineering treats the context window as a scarce, degrading resource — the goal is the smallest set of high-signal tokens that reliably produces the desired behavior, not the largest.

Three techniques cover most long-running agent failures: compaction (summarize and restart), structured note-taking (memory outside the window), and multi-agent delegation (keep exploration noise off the main thread).

How you structure what stays matters too: organizing prompts into clear sections — background, instructions, tool guidance, output format — marked with XML tags or Markdown headers reduces ambiguity about what each chunk of context is for.

questions.prompt: What is the central question context engineering asks, per Anthropic's framing?
options: Which exact words in the prompt sound the most natural
What configuration of context is most likely to generate the model's desired behavior
How to make the context window as large as technically possible
Which fine-tuning dataset best matches the target task
questions.explanation: Context engineering shifts the focus from word choice to curating the whole configuration of context — what's included, what's left out, and how it's structured — so the model's finite attention lands on what matters.

questions.prompt: What does 'compaction' do when an agent's conversation nears the context limit?
options: It deletes a random half of the conversation history
It summarizes the conversation so far and reinitializes a new context window seeded with that summary
It compresses every token using a smaller vocabulary encoding
It pauses the agent until a human manually reviews the transcript
questions.explanation: Compaction replaces the full raw history with a summary in a fresh context window, keeping the window's contents proportional to what's actually still relevant rather than everything that's ever happened.

questions.prompt: Why tune a compaction prompt for recall before precision?
options: Because precision can't be measured reliably
Maximizing recall first ensures no relevant fact from the trace is lost; precision can then trim excess without risking deletion of something the agent still needs
Because recall is computationally cheaper to run than precision
Because a longer summary always improves agent performance regardless of content
questions.explanation: Doing precision first risks aggressively cutting details before you know they're safe to cut. Recall-first guarantees the important facts survive; precision then removes only what's truly superfluous.

references.title: Effective context engineering for AI agents (Anthropic Engineering)

posts.title: LLM-as-a-Verifier: Grading Agents Without a Reward Model
posts.subtitle: A training-free verifier that reads continuous scores off the logits, not a discrete pass/fail.
posts.summary: LLM-as-a-Verifier turns an LLM's own score-token logits into a continuous, calibrated reward — no separate reward model, no fine-tuning. Combined with a tournament algorithm that cuts comparison cost from O(N²) to O(Nk), it sets new best-of-N state of the art on coding, robotics, and medical agent benchmarks.

slides.title: Picking the Best of N Candidate Agent Runs, Cheaply
slides.body: Stanford, UC Berkeley, and NVIDIA researchers built a verifier that scores agent trajectories without training a reward model — just by reading probabilities the base LLM already computes.

slides.title: Discrete Judges Throw Away Information
bullets: Best-of-N sampling generates several candidate solutions to an agentic task (a code patch, a robot action plan, a diagnosis) and needs to pick the best one.
Standard 'LLM-as-judge' prompts a model to output a single discrete label (pass/fail, or a 1-10 score) — noisy, low-resolution, and blind to how confident the model actually was.
Training a dedicated reward model fixes the resolution problem but costs a separate training run per domain, and doesn't transfer across coding, robotics, and medical tasks.

slides.title: Read the Score Off the Logits Themselves
bullets: Instead of asking for one discrete score, the model is prompted to emit a score token from an ordered set (e.g. 1 through 10) — but the verifier keeps the full probability distribution over that set, not just the top pick.
The final reward is the expectation over that distribution: each candidate score token's probability is weighted by its scalar value and summed, producing a continuous number instead of a bucket.
This needs zero extra training: it repurposes logits the base LLM already produces during generation.

slides.title: Three Knobs, Then a Tournament
bullets: Score granularity: more score tokens (finer buckets) means a more precise continuous estimate from the same distribution.
Repeated evaluation (K): re-run the verifier K times per criterion and average, cutting variance from any single sampling.

Criteria decomposition (C): split 'is this good?' into C independent sub-criteria (compiles? passes tests? minimal diff?) scored separately, then combined — easier for the model to judge each piece than the whole at once.

To rank N candidates cheaply, the Probabilistic Pivot Tournament (PPT) skips full pairwise comparison (O(N²)): it does a ring pass over adjacent pairs, picks pivots from the top-k, and concentrates further scoring only on the uncertain top contenders — O(Nk) instead.

slides.title: State of the Art, Not the Ceiling
Worked Example: Picking Among 5 Candidate Patches
bullets: An agent generates 5 different patches for the same bug fix. A discrete judge might tag three of them 'pass' and leave a coin-flip between them.

LLM-as-a-Verifier decomposes the judgment into criteria — does it compile, do tests pass, is the diff minimal, does it match style — and scores each candidate on each criterion as a continuous expectation, not a label.

PPT then runs a ring pass over the 5 candidates, promotes the top contenders as pivots, and spends its remaining comparison budget narrowing between just those — instead of comparing all 10 possible pairs.

slides.title: Where It Still Falls Short
bullets: Training-free means no reward-model training run, but it's not free: K repeated evaluations × C criteria means real extra inference cost per candidate, on top of generating N candidates in the first place.

The gap to the oracle upper bound (86.5% vs 92.1% on Terminal-Bench V2) shows the verifier is still bounded by the base model's own judgment — it can't rank candidates it fundamentally can't tell apart.
Score-token calibration matters: if the underlying model's probabilities over score tokens are poorly calibrated, the 'continuous' reward inherits that miscalibration.

slides.title: Verification as Its Own Scaling Axis
bullets: Reading the full logit distribution over score tokens — not just the top label — turns any instruction-following LLM into a continuous, training-free verifier.
Criteria decomposition and repeated evaluation trade inference compute for ranking precision; the Probabilistic Pivot Tournament keeps that trade affordable at O(Nk) instead of O(N²).

It closes a meaningful chunk of the gap to an oracle ranker across coding, robotics, and medical agent benchmarks — but the oracle gap that remains is a reminder this scales judgment, it doesn't manufacture it.

questions.prompt: What does LLM-as-a-Verifier read to produce a continuous score, instead of a discrete pass/fail label?
options: The full probability distribution over an ordered set of score tokens, weighted into an expectation
The number of tokens in the candidate response
A separately fine-tuned reward model's output layer
The candidate's cosine similarity to a reference solution
questions.explanation: The verifier keeps the whole logit distribution the model already computes over its score tokens and takes the expectation across it, rather than collapsing to the single most-likely label the way standard LLM-as-judge prompting does.

questions.prompt: What problem does the Probabilistic Pivot Tournament (PPT) solve?
options: It trains a reward model faster
It reduces the cost of ranking N candidates from O(N²) pairwise comparisons down to O(Nk)
It compresses the LLM's weights for faster inference
It converts discrete labels into embeddings
questions.explanation: Comparing every candidate against every other candidate scales quadratically. PPT does a cheap ring pass, promotes only the strongest candidates as pivots, and concentrates the remaining comparison budget on those — cutting cost to roughly linear in N.

questions.prompt: On Terminal-Bench V2, LLM-as-a-Verifier scores 86.5% versus an oracle upper bound of 92.1%. What does that gap tell you?
options: The benchmark is broken and the numbers aren't comparable
The verifier is still bounded by the base model's own judgment quality — it can improve ranking, not manufacture insight the model doesn't have
The oracle used a completely different set of candidates
92.1% is actually the verifier's real-world ceiling once repeated evaluation is added
questions.explanation: The oracle represents perfect selection among the same N candidates. The remaining gap shows the verifier can't rank candidates whose quality difference is beyond what the base model can actually discern from its own outputs.

references.title: LLM-as-a-Verifier: A General-Purpose Verification Framework (arXiv:2607.05391)

posts.title: LongCat-2.0: A 1.6T Open Model Trained Without Nvidia
posts.subtitle: Meituan's flagship MoE runs its entire training and serving stack on domestic Chinese ASICs.
posts.summary: Meituan open-sourced LongCat-2.0 under MIT license: a 1.6-trillion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model with ~48B activated parameters per token, native 1M-token context via a new sparse attention scheme, and a training run of 35T+ tokens completed entirely on non-Nvidia ASIC hardware.

slides.title: A Trillion-Parameter Model, Zero Nvidia GPUs
slides.body: Meituan's LongCat-2.0 is proof that frontier-scale pretraining no longer requires the usual H100/A100 supply chain.

slides.title: Why the Hardware Story Matters as Much as the Model
bullets: Export controls have made it harder for Chinese labs to buy the Nvidia and AMD accelerators most frontier training runs depend on.
LongCat-2.0's pretraining — over 35 trillion tokens, millions of accelerator-days — ran entirely on domestic AI ASIC superpods, with no reported rollbacks or irrecoverable loss spikes.
That's as much a systems and infrastructure result as a modeling one: it demonstrates frontier-scale training is reproducible on alternative hardware, not just a one-off GPU cluster.

slides.title: 1.6T Total, ~48B Active — and a New Attention Scheme
bullets: Mixture-of-Experts architecture: 1.6 trillion total parameters, but only about 48 billion activate per token, keeping per-token compute far below the total parameter count.

LongCat Sparse Attention (LSA) adds three pieces on top of prior sparse-indexing work: Streaming-aware Indexing for hardware-aligned memory access, Cross-Layer Indexing that amortizes one indexing pass across several layers, and Hierarchical Indexing with coarse-to-fine scoring.
A 135B-parameter n-gram embedding (carried over from LongCat-Flash-Lite) plus a 3-step multi-token-prediction module let the model draft multiple tokens for speculative decoding.
Native context window: 1 million tokens, trained on hundreds of billions of tokens of long-context data specifically.

slides.title: Benchmark Numbers
What This Looks Like in Practice
bullets: The 1M-token context is large enough to hand the model an entire mid-sized monorepo in a single prompt instead of chunking it — relevant for agentic coding tools that need whole-codebase awareness.
MIT license means a company can fine-tune and redeploy commercially with no royalty or copyleft obligations, unlike more restrictive 'open-weight' licenses some labs use.

Meituan built integrations for Claude Code, OpenClaw, and Hermes agent frameworks, and the model runs via SGLang on both GPU and NPU targets — so adopting it doesn't require abandoning existing agent tooling.

slides.title: What to Watch Before Adopting It
bullets: 1.6T total parameters still means a very large multi-node deployment footprint even though only ~48B activate per token — MoE lowers compute, not memory.
It was trained on ASIC superpods, not commodity Nvidia clusters; downstream fine-tuning or custom serving pipelines built around CUDA-specific tooling may need adaptation.

Sparse attention and MoE routing both introduce failure modes (indexing errors, expert imbalance) that dense models don't have — worth stress-testing on your own long-context workloads before committing.

slides.title: Open, Huge, and Not Built on Nvidia
bullets: LongCat-2.0 pairs a 1.6T/48B-active MoE with a novel sparse attention scheme (LSA) to hit native 1M-token context.
Its biggest signal isn't the benchmark scores — it's that the entire pretraining run happened on non-Nvidia domestic hardware at frontier scale.
MIT licensing and existing agent-framework integrations make it a serious option for teams wanting an open, long-context coding model without per-token API costs.

questions.prompt: How many of LongCat-2.0's 1.6 trillion total parameters activate for a given token?
options: All 1.6 trillion, since it's a dense model
About 48 billion, because it's a Mixture-of-Experts architecture
Exactly 1 billion, fixed regardless of input
It varies token-by-token with no fixed budget
questions.explanation: LongCat-2.0 is a MoE model: routing sends each token to a subset of experts, so only ~48B of the 1.6T total parameters do work on any given token — the rest sit unused for that step.

questions.prompt: What is the most notable infrastructure claim behind LongCat-2.0's release?
options: It was trained using only reinforcement learning, with no pretraining phase
The entire 35T+ token pretraining run was completed on domestic Chinese ASIC hardware, not Nvidia or AMD GPUs
It was trained entirely on a single consumer GPU
Training took less than 24 hours
questions.explanation: Meituan reports pretraining ran on AI ASIC superpods with no Nvidia or AMD accelerators involved — a demonstration that frontier-scale training doesn't strictly require the usual GPU supply chain.

questions.prompt: Why does MoE lowering per-token compute NOT mean LongCat-2.0 is cheap to deploy?
options: Because MoE models require more training data than dense models
Because all 1.6T parameters must still be resident in memory across the deployment, even though only ~48B compute per token
Because MoE models cannot use speculative decoding
Because the license requires paying Meituan per inference call
questions.explanation: Sparse activation reduces FLOPs per token, but every expert still needs to be loaded somewhere in the serving cluster in case routing sends tokens its way — so the memory footprint tracks the full 1.6T parameters, not the 48B active subset.

posts.title: Mixture of Experts: Routing Tokens to Specialists
posts.subtitle: How trillion-parameter models keep per-token compute manageable by only waking up a fraction of themselves.
posts.summary: Mixture-of-Experts replaces a transformer's dense feed-forward block with many specialist sub-networks and a learned router that activates only a handful per token. It's how models scale total parameters into the trillions while keeping per-token compute roughly constant — at the cost of a much larger memory footprint and new failure modes like routing collapse.

slides.title: Not Every Parameter Needs to Fire on Every Token
slides.body: A dense transformer runs every parameter for every token. MoE asks: what if most of the network could sit idle, and a router just picked who's needed?

slides.title: Dense Scaling Hits a Compute Wall
bullets: In a standard transformer, every token passes through the same feed-forward block — so doubling total parameters roughly doubles compute (FLOPs) per token, for every single token processed.
That coupling between 'how much the model knows' and 'how much compute each token costs' is what makes dense trillion-parameter models impractically slow to run.
Labs wanted a way to keep growing total capacity without every token paying for all of it.

slides.title: Replace One Big FFN With Many Small Experts
bullets: MoE swaps the transformer's feed-forward block for a bank of E 'expert' sub-networks — each one is itself a smaller feed-forward network, structurally similar to the block it replaces.
A router (a small learned linear layer plus softmax) looks at each token and outputs a score for every expert; only the top-k highest-scoring experts actually run for that token, usually k=1 or k=2.
The token's output is a weighted combination of just those k experts' outputs — the other E-k experts do zero work for that token.

slides.title: The Router's Two Failure Modes and How to Fix Them
bullets: Left unconstrained, routers tend to collapse: they learn to send most tokens to a small favorite subset of experts, leaving others undertrained and useless — a rich-get-richer dynamic.
The standard fix is an auxiliary load-balancing loss added during training, which penalizes the router for routing too unevenly across experts.

A capacity factor caps how many tokens each expert can accept per batch; tokens that overflow a full expert are either dropped or routed to a fallback, trading a little accuracy for predictable memory use.

Because different experts get different tokens, training and serving MoE at scale means expert parallelism: experts are sharded across devices, and tokens get shuffled to the right device via an all-to-all communication step.

slides.title: Worked Example: LongCat-2.0's Numbers
bullets: Meituan's LongCat-2.0 has 1.6 trillion total parameters split across its expert banks, but its router activates only about 48 billion parameters for any given token.
That's roughly a 33x gap between total capacity and per-token compute — the model 'knows' as much as a 1.6T dense model would, but costs closer to a 48B dense model to run per token.
The catch: all 1.6T parameters still have to live in memory somewhere in the serving cluster, because the router can send the next token to any expert — sparsity saves compute, not memory.

slides.title: Dense vs. MoE
When Dense Still Wins
bullets: If you can't fit all experts in memory across your serving fleet, MoE's compute savings don't help — you're memory-bound, not compute-bound.
Fine-tuning MoE models is trickier: gradient updates only touch the experts a given batch actually routed to, so rare experts can undertrain or drift during fine-tuning.

For small models or latency-critical single-GPU deployments, the all-to-all communication overhead of expert parallelism can erase MoE's compute advantage entirely — dense is simpler and often faster end-to-end.

slides.title: Sparsity Buys Scale, Not a Free Lunch
bullets: MoE decouples total model capacity from per-token compute by routing each token to only k of E experts via a learned gate.
Load-balancing loss and capacity factors exist specifically to stop the router from collapsing onto a favorite few experts.
The tradeoff to remember: MoE saves compute, not memory — every expert still has to be resident somewhere, and that reshapes how you deploy, not just how you train.

questions.prompt: In a Mixture-of-Experts layer with top-k routing, what determines which experts process a given token?
options: All experts always process every token, just with different weights
A learned router scores every expert for that token, and only the top-k highest-scoring experts run
Experts are assigned randomly at each forward pass
The token's position in the sequence fixes which expert handles it
questions.explanation: The router is a small learned layer (typically linear + softmax) that scores each expert per token; only the top-k scoring experts actually execute, and the rest do no work for that token.

questions.prompt: Why is an auxiliary load-balancing loss added when training MoE models?
options: To speed up the softmax computation
To prevent the router from collapsing onto a small favorite subset of experts, leaving the rest undertrained
To reduce the total number of parameters in the model
To convert the model from sparse back to dense during inference
questions.explanation: Without a balancing penalty, routers tend toward a rich-get-richer pattern: experts that get more tokens early on improve faster and attract even more tokens, starving the rest. The auxiliary loss penalizes uneven routing to counter this.

questions.prompt: A model has 1.6T total parameters but only ~48B activate per token. What's the correct way to describe the memory savings from this sparsity?
options: Memory usage drops to roughly 48B parameters, matching compute savings
There are no memory savings — all 1.6T parameters must still be resident, since routing can send tokens to any expert
Memory usage becomes unpredictable and varies token by token
MoE eliminates the need to store expert weights entirely
questions.explanation: Sparsity reduces FLOPs per token, not the model's footprint. Because any token could route to any expert, the full set of experts has to be loaded somewhere across the serving cluster — MoE is a compute optimization, not a memory one.

references.title: Switch Transformers: Scaling to Trillion Parameter Models with Simple and Efficient Sparsity (arXiv:2101.03961)

posts.title: GraphRAG: Retrieval Over a Knowledge Graph, Not Chunks
posts.subtitle: When a question spans an entire corpus, top-k chunk similarity structurally can't answer it.
posts.summary: GraphRAG extracts an entity graph from your corpus, clusters it into hierarchical communities with the Leiden algorithm, and pre-summarizes each one — trading index-time LLM cost for the ability to answer corpus-wide, thematic questions that plain vector retrieval structurally cannot.

slides.title: Vector Search Doesn't Know What It's Missing
slides.body: Top-k similarity finds the chunks that resemble your query. It has no way to reason about the whole corpus at once.

slides.title: Some Questions Are Bigger Than Any One Chunk
bullets: Standard RAG retrieves the top-k chunks most similar to the query embedding: great for 'what does section 4 say about X', useless for 'what are the major recurring themes across this entire corpus'.
Vector similarity has no concept of which entities repeat across documents or how they relate to each other.
Microsoft Research's 2024 GraphRAG paper, 'From Local to Global', was built specifically to answer these corpus-wide, query-focused summarization questions.

slides.title: Build a Knowledge Graph, Then Summarize It in Layers
bullets: Step 1: an LLM extracts entities and relationships from every chunk, building a graph of who or what connects to whom.
Step 2: the Leiden community-detection algorithm partitions the graph into clusters of densely connected entities, hierarchically, from broad clusters down to tight sub-clusters.
Step 3: an LLM pre-generates a summary for every community at every level of the hierarchy, before any user query arrives.

slides.title: Two Search Modes: Local and Global
bullets: Local search: for entity-specific questions, walk the graph outward from matched entities and pull in their neighbors and relationships, like vector RAG but relationship-aware.
Global search: for corpus-wide questions, skip retrieval entirely and map-reduce over the pre-built community summaries themselves.
Global search's expensive part is paid once at indexing time; pre-summarizing communities upfront keeps query-time cost low even for 'summarize everything' questions.

slides.title: Vector RAG vs. GraphRAG

rows: Local: low. Global: map-reduce over summaries

slides.title: 'What Are the Main Power Struggles in This Document Set?'
bullets: Vector RAG returns the handful of chunks most similar to the words 'power struggle', likely missing entities that are never mentioned near that exact phrase.

GraphRAG's global search instead reads every community summary (say, 40 of them), asks each 'what power struggles does this community describe', and reduces the partial answers into one coherent summary.
The result surfaces relationships spanning documents that never appear near each other in raw text, because the graph already connected them at index time.

slides.title: When GraphRAG Is the Wrong Tool
bullets: Indexing cost is real: entity extraction and hierarchical summarization both run an LLM over the entire corpus before a single query is answered.
For a small corpus or narrow fact-lookup use case, plain vector or hybrid search is cheaper and just as accurate — don't build a graph you don't need.
Graph quality depends on extraction quality: noisy or inconsistent entity extraction produces a noisy graph, and errors compound through the community hierarchy.

slides.title: Pre-Summarize Once, Map-Reduce Cheaply
bullets: GraphRAG trades index-time LLM cost for the ability to answer corpus-wide, thematic questions vector search structurally cannot.
The Leiden algorithm's hierarchical communities are the key trick: pre-summarize once, then map-reduce cheaply at query time.
Use local search for entity questions, global search for 'across everything' questions, and reach for plain RAG when you need neither.

questions.prompt: Why can't standard vector RAG reliably answer 'what are the major themes across this entire corpus'?
options: It retrieves only the top-k chunks most similar to the query, with no mechanism to reason about the whole corpus at once
Vector embeddings are too large to store for big corpora
LLMs cannot process more than one retrieved chunk at a time
Vector search only works on structured data, not free text
questions.explanation: Top-k similarity search is inherently local: it finds chunks that resemble the query, not a synthesis of the entire dataset's structure. Global questions need something that summarizes across the whole corpus, which is what GraphRAG's community hierarchy provides.

questions.prompt: What does the Leiden algorithm do in the GraphRAG pipeline?
options: Generates the text embeddings used for vector search
Hierarchically partitions the extracted knowledge graph into communities of densely connected entities
Fine-tunes the underlying LLM on the corpus
Ranks chunks by cosine similarity to the query
questions.explanation: Leiden is a community-detection algorithm: it groups the graph's entities into clusters more connected to each other than to the rest of the graph, recursively, producing a hierarchy from broad to fine-grained communities.

questions.prompt: What's the main reason not to reach for GraphRAG on a small, narrow-topic document set?
options: GraphRAG only works with images, not text
It pays a real index-time cost (entity extraction plus hierarchical summarization) that isn't worth it when plain vector or hybrid search already answers your fact-lookup questions
GraphRAG cannot be combined with an LLM at query time
Knowledge graphs cannot represent relationships between entities
questions.explanation: GraphRAG's value is answering corpus-wide, thematic questions. If your queries are narrow fact lookups, you're paying for graph construction and community summarization without needing the capability it buys you.

posts.title: GenCeption: When Video Generators Learn to See
posts.subtitle: One video-diffusion backbone matches specialist vision models on a fraction of the data.
posts.summary: A new paper repurposes a pretrained text-to-video diffusion backbone as a general-purpose vision model, matching specialist systems on depth, pose, and segmentation tasks with far less task-specific training data — and showing surprising generalization from synthetic to real footage.

slides.title: One Backbone, Five Vision Tasks
slides.body: A video generation model, steered by text, doubles as depth estimator, pose estimator, and segmenter — without a separate architecture for each.

slides.title: Vision Still Trains One Model Per Task
bullets: Depth estimation, segmentation, 3D keypoints, and camera pose each ship as separate specialist architectures with separate training pipelines.
Models like DepthAnything3, SAM3, and VGGT-Omega are excellent at their one job but don't transfer across tasks.
NLP unified around next-token prediction years ago; vision has never had an equivalent generalist recipe.

slides.title: Repurpose the Video Diffusion Backbone Itself
bullets: GenCeption turns a pretrained text-to-video diffusion model into a feed-forward perception model — no new backbone trained from scratch.
A task is just a text instruction ('estimate depth', 'segment the referenced object') that steers the same backbone toward a different output.
No per-task fine-tuning loop: the video generator's own learned priors do most of the work.

slides.title: Why Video Pretraining Transfers to Vision Tasks
bullets: Learning to generate coherent video already forces a model to encode geometry, object permanence, and motion consistency across frames.
GenCeption decodes that representation into task-specific outputs — depth maps, keypoints, masks — instead of denoised video frames.
The vision-language alignment learned from text-to-video captions doubles as instruction-following for the downstream vision task.

slides.title: Matching Specialists With a Fraction of the Data
slides.body: Reported against strong specialist baselines including D4RT and VGGT-Omega.

rows: Reported 7-500x less data, matching or beating both

slides.title: Trained on Synthetic Humans, Tested on Real Animals and Robots
bullets: GenCeption was trained exclusively on synthetic videos of humans.
At test time it was pointed at real-world footage of animals and robots — categories it never saw during training.
It still produced accurate depth and pose estimates, evidence the video-generation prior generalized past its narrow training distribution rather than memorizing it.

slides.title: Where This Approach Falls Short
bullets: A video diffusion backbone is heavier at inference than a small specialist head — don't reach for this if you only need fast, depth-only inference at the edge.
Feed-forward decoding avoids iterative diffusion sampling at inference, but the backbone itself is still large; the cost isn't free.
Results are benchmarked as competitive with strong specialists like SAM3 and DepthAnything3 — notable, not universally superior on every metric.

slides.title: Video Generation Might Be Vision's Next-Token Prediction
bullets: One broad pretraining objective — generate realistic video — turns out to carry most of what several specialist vision tasks need.
The headline result is data efficiency: matching specialists while needing far fewer task-specific labels.
Expect this pattern — repurposing a generative backbone as a perception model — to spread to other modalities next.

questions.prompt: What does GenCeption reuse to build a general-purpose vision model?
options: A pretrained text-to-video diffusion backbone
A brand-new vision transformer trained from scratch
An ensemble of specialist models like SAM3 and DepthAnything3
A large language model fine-tuned on image captions
questions.explanation: GenCeption's core idea is that a video generation model already learned the spatiotemporal priors vision tasks need, so instead of training a new backbone, it repurposes the video diffusion model itself, steered by text instructions.

questions.prompt: What is the headline efficiency result GenCeption reports against specialists like D4RT and VGGT-Omega?
options: It requires 7-500x MORE training data for the same accuracy
It matches or beats them using roughly 7-500x LESS training data
It only works on synthetic data, never on real footage
It ties on benchmarks but runs far slower at inference
questions.explanation: The paper's key efficiency claim is matching specialist performance while needing far less task-specific training data, because the video-generation pretraining already carries most of the necessary visual knowledge.

questions.prompt: What surprising generalization did the paper report?
options: The model refused to run on any data outside its training distribution
A model trained only on synthetic human videos generalized to real footage of animals and robots
The model required separate fine-tuning for every new object category
Performance collapsed entirely on out-of-distribution inputs
questions.explanation: Despite training exclusively on synthetic human videos, GenCeption transferred to real-world, out-of-distribution categories like animals and robots, evidence the learned prior is genuinely general rather than memorized.

posts.title: Grouped-Query Attention: Splitting the Difference
posts.subtitle: How Llama 2, Llama 3, and Mistral shrink the KV cache without eating Multi-Query Attention's quality hit
posts.summary: Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) has query heads share a small number of key/value heads instead of each head keeping its own (Multi-Head Attention) or all heads sharing just one (Multi-Query Attention). It shrinks the KV cache and the memory traffic that bottlenecks autoregressive decoding, while staying much closer to full-attention quality than MQA. Llama 2's 34B/70B, all of Llama 3, and Mistral 7B ship with it — and existing MHA checkpoints can be converted into GQA ones with a small amount of extra pretraining instead of starting over.

slides.title: One Key/Value Head Can Serve a Whole Group of Query Heads
slides.body: Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) sits between two extremes: every query head keeping its own key/value head (Multi-Head Attention), and every query head sharing a single key/value head (Multi-Query Attention). GQA splits query heads into a handful of groups, each sharing one K/V head.

slides.title: Decoding Is Bottlenecked by Memory Traffic, Not Math
slides.body: Generating one token at a time means the GPU does very little compute per step (one new query vector), but it still has to read the entire cached keys and values for every head, every layer, every prior token out of HBM.
bullets: That read, not the matmul, dominates decode latency — it's a classic memory-bandwidth-bound workload, so anything that shrinks the KV cache speeds up every subsequent token
Standard Multi-Head Attention (MHA) caches a separate K and V per head — with H heads, the cache (and the bytes read per step) scales linearly with H

Multi-Query Attention (MQA, Shazeer 2019) collapses all query heads onto one shared K/V head, cutting cache size by roughly a factor of H — but that aggressive sharing measurably hurts output quality and can destabilize training

slides.title: Group the Heads Instead of Merging Them All
slides.body: GQA (Ainslie et al., 2023) partitions the H query heads into G groups, where 1 < G < H. Every query head in a group attends using that group's single shared K/V head. G = H recovers ordinary MHA; G = 1 recovers MQA — GQA is the tunable middle ground.
bullets: Cache size (and memory traffic per decode step) shrinks by a factor of H/G compared to full MHA
Quality degrades gracefully as G shrinks — nowhere near the cliff MQA hits when it forces every head down to one shared K/V
G is a straightforward hyperparameter choice made at pretraining time, constrained only by needing to divide H evenly

slides.title: You Don't Need to Retrain From Scratch
slides.body: The GQA paper's other contribution is a cheap way to convert an already-trained MHA checkpoint instead of pretraining a grouped model from zero.
bullets: 'Uptraining': initialize each group's shared K/V head by mean-pooling the original per-head K/V projections that fall into that group — not by arbitrarily picking one head and discarding the rest
Continue pretraining the converted checkpoint for a small additional slice of the original training compute so the model adapts to the new shared heads
This makes GQA cheap to retrofit onto an existing MHA model family, which is exactly how it was validated on T5-XXL-scale checkpoints

slides.title: MHA vs. GQA vs. MQA

rows: A handful of groups (G), 1 < G < H

slides.title: Llama 2 70B: 64 Query Heads, 8 K/V Heads
slides.body: Llama 2's 34B and 70B models use GQA with 8 K/V head groups; the smaller 7B and 13B models kept plain MHA. Llama 3 extended GQA to every model size, including the 8B, and Mistral 7B (32 query heads, 8 K/V heads) ships it too.
bullets: On 70B, 64 query heads are split into 8 groups of 8 — each group's 8 query heads share one K/V head
That's an 8x smaller KV cache than full MHA would need at the same context length, directly cutting the bytes read from HBM on every decoding step
Because it's an architectural choice baked in at pretraining time, none of this is visible to callers of the model — it just shows up as faster, cheaper serving at long context

slides.title: Where GQA Isn't the Right Call
slides.body: GQA optimizes specifically for the memory-bandwidth wall of autoregressive decoding — it isn't a universal attention upgrade.
bullets: Encoder-only or non-autoregressive workloads that don't grow a KV cache step-by-step see little to no benefit — the problem GQA solves doesn't exist there
It's a pretraining-time architecture decision, not a serving-time toggle — you can't flip an already-deployed MHA model into GQA without the uptraining step (or full retraining)
Push G too low and you approach MQA's quality cliff; picking G is a real tradeoff, not a free lunch — most production models land around 4-8 K/V heads regardless of total head count
For workloads chasing maximum cache compression at long context (128K+), Multi-Head Latent Attention's low-rank latent goes further than GQA, at the cost of needing bespoke kernels

slides.title: The Core Idea, Compressed
bullets: Autoregressive decoding is memory-bandwidth bound: shrinking the KV cache directly speeds up every generated token
GQA groups query heads so each group shares one K/V head — a tunable middle ground between MHA (one K/V head per query head) and MQA (one K/V head total)
Existing MHA checkpoints can be uptrained into GQA by mean-pooling K/V heads per group, then briefly continuing pretraining — no need to start from scratch
It's now the default for open-weight LLMs: Llama 2's larger models, all of Llama 3, and Mistral 7B all ship with GQA

questions.prompt: Why does shrinking the KV cache speed up autoregressive token generation?
options: It reduces the number of floating-point operations needed per token
Decoding one token at a time is memory-bandwidth bound — the GPU spends most of its time reading the cached keys/values, not computing — so a smaller cache means less to read
It lets the model skip the softmax step in attention
It reduces the vocabulary size the model has to search over
questions.explanation: At decode time the compute per step is tiny (one new query vector), but the entire KV cache must be read from memory every step. That read dominates latency, which is why cache size — not FLOPs — is the lever GQA pulls.

questions.prompt: How does GQA relate to Multi-Head Attention (MHA) and Multi-Query Attention (MQA)?
options: GQA is unrelated — it's a completely different mechanism from both
GQA is the special case of MQA used only for encoder models
GQA generalizes both: G groups of shared K/V heads, where G = H recovers MHA and G = 1 recovers MQA
GQA always uses exactly 2 K/V heads regardless of model size
questions.explanation: GQA introduces a tunable number of groups G. Setting G equal to the number of query heads H gives ordinary MHA (every head has its own K/V); setting G to 1 gives MQA (all heads share one K/V). Values in between are GQA proper.

questions.prompt: Llama 2 70B uses 64 query heads split into 8 GQA groups. What does that mean for its KV cache?
options: The cache is 8 times larger than plain MHA would need, because groups add overhead
There is no KV cache at all — GQA eliminates caching entirely
Each of the 8 groups shares one K/V head across its 8 query heads, so the cache is about 8 times smaller than full MHA at the same context length
The cache size is unrelated to the number of groups
questions.explanation: 64 query heads in 8 groups means each K/V head serves 8 query heads. Compared to MHA's one K/V head per query head, that's roughly an 8x reduction in cache size and in the bytes read per decoding step.

posts.title: HNSW: The Graph That Makes Vector Search Fast
posts.subtitle: How a layered graph turns 'compare against everything' into a handful of hops
posts.summary: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is the graph-based algorithm behind most production vector search — it turns a query that would need to touch every stored vector into a handful of hops through a layered graph. Understanding its M, efConstruction, and ef knobs is the difference between a RAG system that's fast and accurate versus one that's slow, bloated, or missing the right chunks.

slides.title: Billions of Vectors, Milliseconds Per Query
slides.body: Every RAG pipeline, semantic search bar, and recommendation feed eventually asks the same question: given this embedding, which stored vectors are closest? HNSW is the graph structure that answers it without scanning everything.

slides.title: Brute-Force Nearest Neighbor Doesn't Scale
slides.body: Exact k-nearest-neighbor search compares your query vector against every stored vector — O(n) distance computations per query.
bullets: At 10K vectors, brute force is fine — sub-millisecond on a CPU.
At 10M+ vectors, comparing against everything turns a query into seconds, not milliseconds.
Distance math (cosine/dot product) is cheap per pair, but it's the count of pairs that kills you at scale.
The fix isn't a faster distance function — it's not comparing against everything.

slides.title: A Graph Built Like a Skip List
slides.body: HNSW (Malkov & Yashunin, 2016) stacks multiple layers of a proximity graph, where nearby vectors are connected by edges. Search starts at a single entry point in the sparsest top layer and greedily walks toward the query.
bullets: Top layers are sparse with long-range 'highway' edges — a few hops cover huge distances in vector space.
Each layer down is denser; the bottom layer (layer 0) contains every vector in the index.
A node's layer assignment is randomized with exponentially decaying probability, so most nodes only exist at layer 0 — same trick a skip list uses for O(log n) lookups.
Search greedily moves to the neighbor closest to the query at each layer, then drops down a layer and repeats until it bottoms out.

slides.title: Three Knobs: M, efConstruction, ef
slides.body: HNSW's behavior is controlled by three parameters you set once at index build time and once (adjustably) at query time.
bullets: M — max bi-directional edges per node per layer. Typical range 16–48; higher M means a denser, more accurate graph but more memory and slower inserts.
efConstruction — how many candidate neighbors are considered while building each node's edges. Typical default ~200; higher means a better-connected graph built more slowly.
ef (efSearch) — how many candidates are tracked while answering a query. Must be ≥ k (the number of results you want); raising it trades latency for recall.
ef is the one knob you can tune per-query without rebuilding the index — it's your live recall/latency dial.

slides.title: HNSW vs. IVF vs. Brute Force
slides.body: No single index is right for every dataset size and memory budget.
bullets: Brute force: exact, zero index-build cost, but query time grows linearly with dataset size.

IVF (inverted file index): clusters vectors with k-means, then searches only the nearest clusters — lower memory than HNSW, often paired with product quantization (IVF-PQ) for billion-scale, but generally lower recall at the same speed.
HNSW: highest recall-per-millisecond of the three at medium-to-large scale, at the cost of holding the full graph (and often full-precision vectors) in memory.

slides.title: 5 Million Chunks, One Query
slides.body: A RAG system embeds 5 million document chunks at 768 dimensions each.
bullets: Brute force: the query vector is compared against all 5M chunk embeddings — on CPU this is a multi-second scan, unacceptable for an interactive chat response.

With HNSW: the query enters at the top layer's single entry point, takes a few long hops toward the right neighborhood, drops a layer, refines, and repeats — touching a few hundred candidates total instead of 5 million.
Result: the same lookup drops to tens of milliseconds, fast enough to sit inside a chat turn alongside the LLM call itself.
This is why pgvector, Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, and FAISS all ship HNSW as a default or recommended index type for this exact workload.

slides.title: The Tradeoffs Nobody Puts on the Slide
slides.body: HNSW is fast, not free — three costs matter in production.
bullets: Memory: every node stores M (2M at layer 0) edges on top of the raw vector, so the index can run 1.5–2x the size of the raw embeddings — painful at hundreds of millions of vectors.
Deletes are awkward: removing a node can disconnect its neighbors' paths, so most implementations tombstone (mark-deleted) rather than truly remove, and periodically rebuild to reclaim space.

It's approximate: ef controls a recall/latency tradeoff, not a guarantee — HNSW can miss the true nearest neighbor, which matters if your RAG system needs the single most relevant chunk, not 'one of the close ones'.
Build time grows with efConstruction and M — reindexing a large, frequently-updated corpus is not instant.

slides.title: Takeaways
slides.body: What to remember when you're the one setting up the vector index.
bullets: HNSW trades memory for speed by replacing a linear scan with a layered graph walk — logarithmic-ish hops instead of comparing against every vector.
M and efConstruction are index-build-time tradeoffs (memory/build speed vs. graph quality); ef is your per-query recall/latency dial.

Reach for HNSW when you have millions of vectors and need low-latency, high-recall lookups with memory to spare — reach for IVF-PQ when memory is the constraint at billion-scale, and brute force when the dataset is small enough that 'exact' costs you nothing.
Deletes and heavy churn are HNSW's weak point — plan for periodic rebuilds if your corpus changes fast.

questions.prompt: In HNSW, what does the query-time parameter `ef` (efSearch) primarily control?
options: The number of graph layers built
The tradeoff between search recall and query latency
The dimensionality of the stored vectors
How many threads the index build uses
questions.explanation: ef sets how many candidate nodes are tracked while searching. A higher ef examines more candidates before returning results, improving the odds of finding the true nearest neighbors at the cost of a slower query — it's the dial you turn per-query without rebuilding anything.

questions.prompt: Why does HNSW use multiple graph layers instead of one flat graph?
options: To reduce total memory usage to near zero
It mimics a skip list — sparse top layers give long 'highway' jumps toward the right region before the dense bottom layer refines the result
GPUs require data to be split into layers to process it
It's required to guarantee exact, 100% recall
questions.explanation: Each node's presence in upper layers is randomized with exponentially decaying probability, so most nodes only live at layer 0. That mirrors how a skip list gets logarithmic search: a few long jumps at the top, then fine-grained steps near the bottom.

questions.prompt: What's a real limitation of HNSW compared to brute-force exact search?
options: It cannot run on CPUs, only GPUs
It doesn't support embeddings below 128 dimensions
It's approximate — increasing ef improves recall but never guarantees the true nearest neighbor is found
It requires labeled training data before it can index vectors
questions.explanation: HNSW is an Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) method. It's dramatically faster than brute force, but the graph walk can settle on a local optimum and miss the true closest vector — a real risk if your application needs the single best match rather than 'one of the close ones'.

posts.title: Long-Horizon-Terminal-Bench: 29 Tasks No Model Solves
posts.subtitle: Give an agent 90 minutes and hundreds of steps in a real terminal, and the leaderboard flips
posts.summary: Long-Horizon-Terminal-Bench (LHTB) grades 21 frontier models on 46 stateful terminal tasks that take up to 90 minutes and hundreds of steps, using hidden rebuild-from-artifact verifiers instead of self-reported progress. Even the best model, Grok 4.5, solves only 13 of 46 tasks outright, and 29 tasks were never solved by any model — a sharp contrast to how capable these same agents look on short, single-shot benchmarks.

slides.title: Most Benchmarks Test a Sprint, Not a Shift
slides.body: Long-Horizon-Terminal-Bench (LHTB) drops agents into containerized terminal environments and grades them over hundreds of steps and up to 90 minutes per task — closer to a real workday than a single prompt-response exchange.
bullets: 46 tasks across 8 categories: software engineering, scientific computing, energy systems modeling, security challenges, research reproduction, multimodal analysis, interactive games, professional workflows
21 frontier models evaluated, each given a 90-minute budget per task
Built by Zongxia Li and collaborators; dataset and leaderboard are public (Apache 2.0)

slides.title: Self-Reported Progress Is Easy to Fake
slides.body: Short terminal benchmarks typically check the final state once, at the end of a single short episode — which rewards agents for looking done rather than being done, and says nothing about whether they can sustain useful work over a long, messy run.
bullets: Prior terminal benchmarks run far shorter episodes — LHTB agents average 231 episodes and 9.9M tokens per task, an order of magnitude more than earlier suites
A model that plans well for 10 steps can still drift, loop, or lose track of state by step 150
Binary pass/fail grading hides *how far* an agent actually got before failing

slides.title: Hidden Verifiers That Rebuild From Artifacts
slides.body: Instead of trusting an agent's own claim of success, LHTB uses hidden verifiers that rebuild and inspect the actual artifacts an agent produced — files, running services, simulation outputs — and grade fine-grained subtasks along the way.
bullets: Each of the 46 tasks is decomposed into graded subtasks, so partial progress earns partial credit instead of an all-or-nothing score
Verifiers run independently of the agent's transcript, so an agent can't game the score by just saying it succeeded
This dense reward signal is what makes the benchmark usable for both evaluation and future RL training

slides.title: The Leaderboard Looks Different at 90 Minutes
slides.body: Cost-efficiency and raw capability don't move together — the cheapest model in this comparison also won on both reward and tasks solved.

slides.title: Where a Run Actually Falls Apart
slides.body: Take an energy-systems-modeling task: the agent has to configure a simulation, run it, read numeric output, adjust parameters, and re-run — dozens of times — before the final artifact passes verification.
bullets: Early steps (environment setup, first simulation run) succeed easily and look like fast progress
Failure typically shows up mid-run: the agent misreads an intermediate result, 'corrects' a parameter that was already right, and compounds the error over the next 50 steps
The hidden rebuild-from-artifact verifier catches this even though the agent's own transcript reads as confident and on-track

slides.title: The Headline Number: 29 of 46, Unsolved by Anyone
slides.body: Across all 21 models, 29 of the 46 tasks were never solved by a single one — and roughly 55% of all model-by-task runs scored a reward below 0.25.
bullets: The strongest models solve only ~28% of tasks at a strict pass threshold
This isn't a capability gap that scales away with a bigger model alone — it's specifically the sustained, multi-hundred-step execution that breaks down
It's a caution against reading short-benchmark leaderboards as a proxy for 'can this agent do real autonomous work'

slides.title: Takeaways
slides.body: LHTB is a reminder that 'agentic' capability isn't one number — a model that tops a short-task leaderboard can still fail most long-horizon work.
bullets: 46 tasks, 8 categories, 90-minute budgets, graded by hidden verifiers that rebuild artifacts rather than trust self-reports
Grok 4.5 leads on both reward and tasks solved, and at the lowest cost per task among the top three
29 of 46 tasks remain unsolved by every one of 21 tested models — long-horizon terminal work is still largely unmastered
Dense, subtask-level grading (not just pass/fail) is what makes this useful for diagnosing exactly where an agent's run goes wrong

questions.prompt: What makes LHTB's grading different from typical short terminal benchmarks?
options: It only checks whether the agent's final chat message claims success
It uses hidden verifiers that rebuild and inspect actual artifacts, with dense subtask-level credit
It grades purely on how fast the agent responds
It only tests agents on multiple-choice questions
questions.explanation: LHTB verifiers rebuild from the actual artifacts an agent produced and grade fine-grained subtasks, so partial progress earns partial credit and the score can't be gamed by a confident-sounding transcript.

questions.prompt: According to the results, which model had both the highest mean reward and the lowest cost per task among the top three?
options: Claude Opus 4.8
Claude Sonnet 5
Grok 4.5
All three tied
questions.explanation: Grok 4.5 topped the leaderboard with 0.505 mean reward and 13/46 tasks solved, at $11.19 per task — cheaper than both Claude Sonnet 5 ($60.37) and Claude Opus 4.8 ($39.11), which scored lower on both reward and solve count.

questions.prompt: What does the finding that 29 of 46 tasks were never solved by any of the 21 tested models suggest?
options: The benchmark's tasks are unsolvable by design and therefore not useful
Current frontier agents still struggle specifically with sustained, multi-hundred-step autonomous execution, not just raw reasoning
All 21 models are equally weak at every kind of task
The benchmark only measures token efficiency
questions.explanation: The gap shows up specifically as run length grows — models that look strong on short tasks lose the thread over hundreds of steps, exposing a distinct capability gap in sustained execution rather than in one-shot reasoning.

references.title: Long-Horizon-Terminal-Bench: Testing the Limits of Agents on Long-Horizon Terminal Tasks with Dense Reward-Based Grading (arXiv:2607.08964)
