posts.title: Mamba: The Linear-Time Alternative to Attention
posts.subtitle: A recurrent state that decides what to remember, token by token — no quadratic attention required
posts.summary: Mamba replaces self-attention with a selective state space model: a recurrence whose parameters change per input token, letting it decide what to keep or forget. That buys linear-time, constant-memory-per-step inference and roughly 5x the throughput of a same-size Transformer, at the cost of exact long-range recall — which is why 2026 production models increasingly mix SSM layers with a few attention layers rather than choosing one architecture outright.

slides.title: What If the Model Never Re-Read the Whole Sequence?
slides.body: Self-attention re-compares every new token against every previous token, which is exactly why it's powerful and exactly why it's expensive. Mamba, introduced by Albert Gu and Tri Dao in December 2023, replaces that comparison with a compressed running state updated one token at a time.
bullets: No attention matrix at all — a recurrent hidden state carries everything the model has decided is worth remembering
Selective: unlike older state space models, Mamba's update parameters are computed from the current input, not fixed
The tradeoff is the whole story: constant-size memory instead of a growing cache, in exchange for imperfect exact recall

slides.title: Attention's Bill Comes Due at Inference Time
slides.body: Self-attention costs O(n²) compute in sequence length n, and every generated token requires holding a growing key-value (KV) cache in memory — the cache scales linearly with context length and quickly dominates GPU memory on long contexts.
bullets: A 100K-token context means the KV cache alone can run into tens of gigabytes, on top of model weights
Prefill (processing the prompt) is quadratic; a 10x longer prompt costs roughly 100x more compute
Techniques like GQA and KV-cache quantization shrink the cache but don't remove the fundamental linear-with-context memory growth

slides.title: Selectivity: Let the Input Decide What Persists
slides.body: Classic state space models (like S4) update a hidden state with fixed matrices — the same rule applied to every token regardless of content. Mamba makes the update matrices functions of the current token, so the model can choose, input by input, what to carry forward and what to discard.
bullets: A token that's clearly irrelevant (filler, a repeated pattern) can be given near-zero weight in the state update
A token carrying a fact the model will need later can be weighted to persist strongly
This selectivity is what lets a fixed-size state substitute for attention's exact, unbounded lookup — most of the time
A hardware-aware parallel scan algorithm makes this recurrence run efficiently on GPUs, despite recurrence usually being sequential and slow

slides.title: The Mamba Block
slides.body: A Mamba block fuses a gated MLP path with the selective SSM path in a single block, repeated to form the full model — there's no separate attention sub-layer and no feed-forward sub-layer stacked afterward the way a Transformer block has both.
bullets: Input projection expands the model dimension, splits into an SSM path and a gating path
The SSM path runs the selective recurrence; the gate (via a SiLU activation) controls how much of that output passes through
Simpler layer count per block than a Transformer's attention+FFN pairing, which is part of why it's cheaper per step

slides.title: Mamba vs. Transformer Attention

rows: Mamba-3B matches Transformers ~2x its size on language modeling

slides.title: A Million-Token Log File
slides.body: Say you're streaming a 1M-token application log through a model to summarize incidents as they happen. A Transformer's KV cache at that length becomes enormous and prefill cost explodes; a Mamba-style model processes it in roughly linear time with a hidden state that doesn't grow past a fixed size.
bullets: Good fit: the task is about tracking evolving state and summarizing trends, not pinpointing one exact line by verbatim match
Bad fit: 'quote the exact error message from line 4,213' — a task that needs precise verbatim retrieval is exactly where fixed-size state can lose fidelity that a full KV cache wouldn't
This is the practical line between when SSMs help and when they quietly cost you accuracy

slides.title: Why Pure SSMs Aren't the Default Yet
slides.body: The same fixed-size state that makes Mamba cheap is also its weak point on tasks that need precise, exact recall of something mentioned much earlier — the classic failure mode is 'needle in a haystack' and induction-head-style copying, where attention's exact KV lookup has a real edge.
bullets: A fixed-size state must compress the past — some information is necessarily lost, unlike attention which can in principle look back at any exact prior token
This is why most 2026 production long-context models are hybrids: a majority of SSM layers for cheap throughput, interleaved with a minority of full-attention layers for exact recall
Training and tooling maturity also lag — the Transformer ecosystem (kernels, quantization tricks, serving frameworks) is far more battle-tested

slides.title: Takeaways
slides.body: Mamba shows that attention isn't the only way to model sequences well — but it trades exact recall for speed, which is why hybrids, not pure SSMs, dominate in practice.
bullets: Selective SSMs make the recurrence update input-dependent, letting a fixed-size state substitute for attention's exact lookup most of the time
Linear-time compute and constant per-step memory give roughly 5x the inference throughput of a same-size Transformer
The cost is weaker exact long-range recall — verbatim retrieval and needle-in-haystack tasks favor full attention
Most production long-context models in 2026 hybridize: mostly SSM layers, with a handful of attention layers mixed in

questions.prompt: What makes Mamba's state space model 'selective', unlike earlier SSMs like S4?
options: It uses attention internally to select tokens
Its update matrices are computed from the current input token instead of being fixed
It selects which GPU to run on for best performance
It randomly drops tokens during training
questions.explanation: Earlier SSMs like S4 apply the same fixed update rule to every token. Mamba computes its update parameters from the current input, so the model can decide, per token, what to keep in its running state and what to discard.

questions.prompt: Why does Mamba scale better than a Transformer at inference time on long sequences?
options: It uses a bigger KV cache to store more context
It has more parameters per layer
Its compute scales linearly (not quadratically) and its per-step memory is a fixed-size state rather than a growing cache
It skips generating some tokens entirely
questions.explanation: Transformer self-attention costs O(n²) compute and needs a KV cache that grows with context length. Mamba's recurrence is O(n) and keeps a fixed-size hidden state per step, which is why it reports roughly 5x higher inference throughput.

questions.prompt: Where does a pure SSM like Mamba tend to underperform a Transformer?
options: Streaming summarization of very long logs
Tasks needing exact, verbatim recall of something stated much earlier in the sequence (needle-in-haystack style)
Any task shorter than 1,000 tokens
Audio and genomics modeling
questions.explanation: A fixed-size hidden state must compress the past, so precise recall of an exact earlier detail can be lossy compared to attention's ability to look back at any specific prior token — which is why hybrid SSM+attention architectures are common in production.

references.title: Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces (arXiv:2312.00752)
state-spaces/mamba — official implementation

posts.title: Continuous Batching: Ending the GPU Bubble in LLM Serving
posts.subtitle: Why waiting for the longest reply in a batch was wasting most of your GPU
posts.summary: Static batching forces a GPU to wait for every sequence in a batch to finish before starting the next one, even though requests finish at wildly different times. Continuous (iteration-level) batching fixes this by admitting new requests into a running batch at every decoding step, which is why serving engines like vLLM and TensorRT-LLM built it into their schedulers.

slides.title: Continuous Batching: Ending the GPU Bubble in LLM Serving
slides.body: A scheduling trick, not a model change, that turned idle GPU cycles into serving throughput — now standard in every serious LLM inference engine.

slides.title: Static Batching Waits for the Slowest Request
slides.body: Group requests into a fixed batch, run the forward pass step by step, and don't touch the batch again until every sequence has produced an end-of-sequence token.
bullets: A batch of 16 requests where 15 finish in 30 tokens and 1 runs to 800 tokens: the GPU keeps recomputing padding for the 15 finished slots the whole time
New requests queue up outside the batch even while most of its slots sit idle
GPU utilization drops sharply near the tail of every batch — this is the classic 'bubble' problem also seen in pipeline-parallel training

slides.title: Schedule by Iteration, Not by Batch
slides.body: Continuous batching (also called dynamic or in-flight batching) makes the scheduling unit one decoding step instead of one whole batch. The scheduler checks in after every single forward pass.
bullets: The moment a sequence emits its EOS token, its slot is freed and immediately handed to the next queued request
The 'batch' is really a live set that's re-composed every iteration, not a fixed group that runs start-to-finish together
First formalized as 'iteration-level scheduling' in the Orca serving system (OSDI 2022), now the default scheduling model in vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and TGI

slides.title: It Needs a Memory Manager That Can Keep Up
slides.body: Resizing the batch every step means the KV cache has to grow and shrink every step too — which is exactly the problem PagedAttention was built to solve.
bullets: Without paged KV cache allocation, admitting a new request mid-batch would require costly memory defragmentation
Continuous batching is the scheduling policy; PagedAttention is the memory layout underneath it — they're separate ideas that happen to be near-mandatory partners
This is why vLLM ships both together rather than either alone

slides.title: The Throughput Gap Is Not Small
slides.body: Because idle slots are refilled instead of held empty, effective GPU utilization rises across the whole serving window, not just per-request latency.
bullets: Widely-cited industry benchmarks (Anyscale's 2023 vLLM writeup) reported up to roughly 23x higher throughput over naive static batching on realistic chat-length workloads
The gap narrows when request lengths are uniform (little tail-waiting to reclaim) and widens when output lengths vary a lot

rows: As soon as any slot frees
GPU idle time from short finishers

slides.title: Worked Example: A Mixed Batch of 4
slides.body: Requests A and B ask short factual questions (~20 tokens). Requests C and D ask for long explanations (~400 tokens). All four arrive together.
bullets: Static batching: GPU processes all 4 in lockstep; A and B's slots sit computing nothing useful for ~380 extra steps while C and D finish
Continuous batching: at step ~20, A and B finish and exit; two new incoming requests E and F are slotted in immediately to fill their spots
Net effect: the GPU never runs a step with unused capacity it could've given to a waiting request

slides.title: Where It Falls Short
slides.body: Continuous batching is a serving-engine feature, not something you can bolt onto any inference loop, and it introduces new failure modes.
bullets: Requires an engine built for iteration-level scheduling — a plain Hugging Face `generate()` loop doesn't do this automatically
A flood of very long requests can still starve short ones if the scheduler has no fairness/priority policy
Prefill (processing the prompt) is compute-heavy and decode (generating tokens) is memory-bandwidth-heavy; mixing them in the same batch without care (no 'chunked prefill') can stall ongoing decodes
Doesn't help if your bottleneck is elsewhere — e.g., a slow tokenizer, network I/O, or a single-request low-QPS workload with no queue to backfill from

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Static batching wastes GPU cycles waiting for a batch's longest sequence to finish
Continuous batching reschedules at every decode step, backfilling freed slots immediately
It pairs with paged KV-cache memory management (PagedAttention) to handle the constant resizing
It's an inference-engine property (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, TGI) — not something a naive generation loop gets for free
Biggest wins come with high request-length variance and a real queue of waiting requests to backfill from

questions.prompt: What GPU inefficiency does continuous batching primarily target?
options: Slow model weight loading at server startup
Idle GPU slots left by requests that finish early while a static batch waits for its longest sequence
High network latency between client and server
Large tokenizer vocabulary lookup time
questions.explanation: In static batching the whole batch is locked together until every sequence hits EOS, so slots for short requests sit idle recomputing nothing useful. Continuous batching frees and refills those slots every decode step instead of waiting for the batch to fully drain.

questions.prompt: How does continuous batching relate to PagedAttention?
options: They're competing techniques and serving engines must pick one
PagedAttention replaced continuous batching in modern engines
Continuous batching is a scheduling policy (which requests run each step); PagedAttention is a KV-cache memory layout — they're complementary and usually deployed together
Continuous batching only applies to CPU inference, PagedAttention only to GPU
questions.explanation: They solve different layers of the same problem: continuous batching decides admission/eviction every step, while PagedAttention gives the KV cache a paged layout so growing and shrinking the batch every step doesn't require expensive memory defragmentation.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a genuine limitation of continuous batching?
options: It requires retraining the underlying model
It only works when batch size is exactly 1
It needs an inference engine built for iteration-level scheduling, and without a fairness policy long requests can starve short ones
It eliminates the need for a KV cache entirely
questions.explanation: Continuous batching is a serving-engine capability, not a free property of any generation loop — you need scheduler support, and mismanaged admission can let long-running requests crowd out shorter ones without an explicit fairness/priority policy.

references.title: How continuous batching enables 23x throughput in LLM inference while reducing p50 latency (Anyscale)

posts.title: J-Space: The Hidden Workspace Inside Claude
posts.subtitle: A Jacobian-based lens finds a tiny subspace that acts like a global workspace for reasoning
posts.summary: Anthropic built the Jacobian lens (J-lens), a tool that reads a hidden state's future output tendencies instead of its current content. Using it, they found J-space: a sub-10%-of-variance subspace of 10-25 active directions that, when ablated, breaks multi-step reasoning while leaving fluent text generation intact.

slides.title: J-Space: The Hidden Workspace Inside Claude
slides.body: Anthropic found a tiny internal subspace that behaves like a functional 'global workspace' for a language model's reasoning — and built a new lens to see it.

slides.title: Feature Dictionaries Show What's There, Not What's Coming
slides.body: Sparse-autoencoder features (like Anthropic's earlier Scaling Monosemanticity work) label what a hidden state currently represents. They don't say what that state is about to make the model do next.
bullets: Static feature reads miss the model's evolving 'disposition' toward future tokens
You want a probe that reads a hidden state's trajectory, not just its snapshot content
Anthropic's answer: the Jacobian lens (J-lens), published July 6, 2026 on Transformer Circuits

slides.title: The Jacobian Lens: Transport, Then Unembed
slides.body: J-lens estimates J_l = the average Jacobian of the final-layer state with respect to a hidden state at layer l, computed empirically over a generic web-text corpus.
bullets: Linearly transport a hidden state h through J_l into the final-layer basis
Apply the model's own unembedding to get a ranked vocabulary read-out
Fit on ~1,000 sequences of 128 tokens; the signal saturates fast — about 100 prompts is already usable
Result: a token-level answer to 'what is this activation currently disposed to make the model eventually say?'

slides.title: J-Space: Under 10% of Variance, Fewer Than 25 Directions
slides.body: Applying J-lens across layers, Anthropic isolated a small set of directions that behave like a shared workspace for the whole model — not something anyone trained it to build.
bullets: Only 10–25 active J-lens directions carry the model's 'working' content at once
Together they account for under 10% of total activation variance
The subspace emerged spontaneously during pretraining, unprompted by any objective for it
Echoes Global Workspace Theory: many processes run in parallel, but only a small 'spotlight' gets broadcast everywhere

slides.title: The ASCII Face That Gives Away 'Nose' Early
slides.body: In the companion walkthrough notebook, Anthropic feeds the model an ASCII-art face prompt and reads out mid-layer activations with J-lens.
bullets: The word 'nose' never appears anywhere in the prompt text
Mid-layer J-lens readouts already rank 'nose' highly — before the model has written it
That's the functional signature the paper is after: content held and reportable before it's verbalized
It's the same property Global Workspace Theory calls 'access consciousness' — used here only as a functional description, not a claim about experience

slides.title: Ablate J-Space: Shallow Tasks Survive, Composition Doesn't
slides.body: Suppressing the J-space directions leaves simple pattern-matching intact but collapses anything requiring multiple reasoning steps.

slides.title: What J-Space Doesn't Prove
slides.body: The result is a carefully scoped functional finding, not a claim about machine consciousness — and it comes with real methodological caveats.
bullets: J-lens reads a linear approximation (an averaged Jacobian) of a genuinely non-linear system — fidelity depends on the fitting corpus
'Disposed to say' is a correlational read-out, not a guarantee of causal control over the output
Anthropic frames the Global Workspace Theory parallel explicitly as functional, not phenomenal
Every model/layer needs its own J_l estimate — a real, if cheap, calibration step before you can use the lens

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: J-lens reads what a hidden state is disposed to produce later, not just what it encodes now
J-space is small (10–25 directions, <10% of variance) but load-bearing for anything needing composition
Ablating it breaks reasoning and analogy while leaving shallow classification and fluency untouched
It's a functional analogy to a 'global workspace' — useful for engineering, not a verdict on machine experience

questions.prompt: What does the Jacobian lens (J-lens) actually decode from a hidden state?
options: The raw activation vector at that layer
An averaged Jacobian-based linear transport of the hidden state into the final-layer basis, then unembedded into vocabulary
The attention weights feeding into that layer
A human-labeled interpretable feature dictionary
questions.explanation: J-lens computes J_l, the average Jacobian of the final layer with respect to layer l over a text corpus, transports the hidden state through it, then applies the model's own unembedding — producing a vocabulary-level read-out of what the state is currently disposed to make the model eventually say.

questions.prompt: What happened when Anthropic ablated J-space in Claude?
options: The model stopped producing fluent text entirely
Shallow tasks like multiple-choice QA and sentiment classification kept working, but multi-hop reasoning, analogy, and translation collapsed
All tasks improved because noise was removed from the residual stream
The model's effective vocabulary size shrank
questions.explanation: The ablation was selective: tasks that only need shallow pattern-matching survived largely intact, while anything requiring flexible multi-step composition broke down — which is what makes J-space look load-bearing rather than incidental.

questions.prompt: Roughly how much of the model's total activation variance does J-space occupy?
options: Under 10%, concentrated in about 10–25 active directions
Around 50%, spread evenly across every layer
Nearly 100%, since it spans the whole residual stream
A fixed single direction per layer
questions.explanation: J-space is deliberately small — a handful of directions carrying under a tenth of the model's activation variance — which is part of why its outsized effect on reasoning ability is notable.

references.title: Verbalizable Representations Form a Global Workspace in Language Models — Transformer Circuits
A global workspace in language models — Anthropic
anthropics/jacobian-lens — official GitHub repository

posts.title: Sparse Autoencoders: Unpacking Superposition
posts.subtitle: How Anthropic pulled millions of monosemantic concepts out of a black box
posts.summary: Individual neurons in an LLM often fire for several unrelated concepts because models pack more features than they have dimensions (superposition). Sparse autoencoders untangle this by learning an overcomplete, sparsely-active dictionary of directions — and Anthropic scaled the technique to 34 million interpretable, steerable features in a production model.

slides.title: Sparse Autoencoders: Unpacking Superposition
slides.body: One neuron, a dozen unrelated meanings. Sparse autoencoders are how interpretability researchers untangle what a model's activations are actually doing.

slides.title: One Neuron, a Dozen Unrelated Meanings
slides.body: Models represent vastly more concepts than they have neurons or residual-stream dimensions, so they compress many sparse, rarely-co-occurring features onto the same directions.
bullets: This is superposition: the model's way of cramming more features than it has room for
The result is polysemanticity — a single neuron firing for, say, both legal contracts and unrelated concepts
You can't read a model's 'thoughts' off individual neurons if each one means several things at once

slides.title: Train an Overcomplete, Sparse Dictionary on the Activations
slides.body: A sparse autoencoder (SAE) is trained directly on a model's internal activations — not on its outputs.
bullets: The encoder projects each activation into a much higher-dimensional code (more feature directions than the residual stream has dimensions)
An L1 sparsity penalty pushes almost all of those directions to zero for any single input
The decoder reconstructs the original activation from just the handful of features left active
Anthropic first showed this works on a one-layer toy transformer in 'Towards Monosemanticity' (2023)

slides.title: Scaling Monosemanticity: 34 Million Features Out of Claude 3 Sonnet
slides.body: In 2024, Anthropic scaled the same recipe from a toy model to a production model's middle-layer residual stream.
bullets: Trained SAEs of increasing size — roughly 1M, 4M, and 34M features — on Claude 3 Sonnet
Extracted features that are multilingual and multimodal, firing for the same concept across languages and even images despite text-only training
Found features for both concrete entities (specific landmarks) and abstract concepts (sarcasm, bugs in code)

slides.title: Golden Gate Claude: Clamping One Feature
slides.body: Anthropic took a single discovered feature — one that fires for the Golden Gate Bridge — and clamped its activation to an artificially high, fixed value.
bullets: The model began working the Golden Gate Bridge into answers on unrelated topics
This showed the features aren't just descriptive labels — they're causally steerable levers on behavior
The same clamping mechanism generalizes: amplify a feature, watch the corresponding behavior get stronger

slides.title: Raw Neuron vs. SAE Feature
The Costs: Reconstruction Error, Splitting, Dead Features
slides.body: Scaling sparse autoencoders isn't a free lunch — it trades one set of problems for another.
bullets: Reconstruction is never perfect — some activation information is left unexplained by the learned features
As dictionary size grows, single features tend to 'split' into several more specific sub-features, with no obvious stopping point
Many latent directions in a large dictionary never fire on real data — dead features that add cost without adding insight
Training a 34M-feature SAE means storing and processing activation data at a scale that rivals the base model's own training cost

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Superposition is why individual neurons are often polysemantic — models pack in more features than they have room for
Sparse autoencoders recover monosemantic features by learning an overcomplete, sparsely-active dictionary from activations
Anthropic scaled this from a toy one-layer model to 34 million features on Claude 3 Sonnet
Features aren't just labels — clamping one (Golden Gate Claude) causally steers model behavior, at the cost of reconstruction error and dictionary bloat

questions.prompt: Why do individual neurons in an LLM often respond to several unrelated concepts?
options: They are undertrained relative to the rest of the model
Superposition: the model has far more concepts to represent than available dimensions, so it compresses multiple sparse features onto shared directions
Concepts are assigned to neurons at random during initialization and never change
Neurons only encode syntax, never semantics
questions.explanation: Superposition lets a model represent more features than it has neurons by relying on those features rarely co-occurring — the tradeoff is that any single neuron ends up polysemantic, firing for multiple unrelated concepts.

questions.prompt: What does the L1 sparsity penalty do during sparse autoencoder training?
options: Forces the decoder to reconstruct the input with zero error
Encourages most of the overcomplete dictionary's features to stay inactive (zero) for any given input, so only a handful explain each activation
Reduces the number of layers in the underlying base model
Increases the learning rate as training progresses
questions.explanation: The L1 penalty is what pushes the learned code toward sparsity — without it, the overcomplete dictionary would just learn a dense, uninterpretable re-encoding of the same superposed activations.

questions.prompt: What did clamping the 'Golden Gate Bridge' feature demonstrate?
options: That SAE features are purely descriptive and can't affect model output
That the sparse autoencoder had failed to train properly
That artificially amplifying a single feature's activation causally steers the model's behavior and output
That the bridge concept only exists in image-only models
questions.explanation: Golden Gate Claude worked the bridge into unrelated answers once that feature was clamped high — proof that SAE features are causal levers on behavior, not just passive labels for interpretability dashboards.

references.title: Towards Monosemanticity: Decomposing Language Models With Dictionary Learning — Transformer Circuits
Scaling Monosemanticity: Extracting Interpretable Features from Claude 3 Sonnet — Transformer Circuits

posts.title: Multi-Head Latent Attention: Compressing the KV Cache
posts.subtitle: DeepSeek's trick for caching one small latent vector instead of a full set of keys and values
posts.summary: Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) shrinks the KV cache by storing a single low-rank latent vector per token instead of separate keys and values for every attention head, then reconstructing per-head K/V on the fly. DeepSeek-V2 reported this cuts KV cache size by about 93% versus standard multi-head attention while matching or beating its quality — at the cost of a trickier RoPE implementation and no easy retrofit onto existing checkpoints.

slides.title: One Latent Vector Replaces a Whole Set of Keys and Values
slides.body: Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA), introduced in DeepSeek-V2, compresses the per-token KV cache into a single low-rank latent vector instead of storing separate keys and values for every attention head.

slides.title: The KV Cache Is What Actually Runs Out of Memory
slides.body: Every token generated has to keep its keys and values in GPU memory for every later token to attend back to. That cache — not the model weights — is usually what limits context length and batch size at serving time.
bullets: Standard multi-head attention (MHA) caches a separate key and value vector per head, per layer, per token — the cache grows linearly with heads × head_dim × layers × sequence length
At 128K context, that cache can dwarf the weights themselves, forcing smaller batches and lower throughput
Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) shrinks the cache by sharing K/V across groups of heads, but coarser sharing measurably hurts quality

slides.title: Cache a Compressed Latent, Reconstruct K/V on the Fly
slides.body: Instead of caching per-head keys and values, MLA down-projects the hidden state into a single low-dimensional latent vector c_t. That latent is what gets cached. At attention time, learned up-projection matrices reconstruct the per-head keys and values from it.
bullets: The up-projection matrices can be algebraically folded into the query and output projections ('absorption'), so reconstruction adds no extra matmul at inference time
DeepSeek-V2's technical report puts the resulting KV cache at roughly 93% smaller than standard MHA, at quality that matched or exceeded full MHA on their benchmarks
Queries can optionally be low-rank compressed too — that saves activation memory during training but isn't needed for the inference-time cache win

slides.title: RoPE Doesn't Compress Cleanly, So It Gets Decoupled
slides.body: Rotary position embeddings rotate each head's key vector by a position-dependent angle. Applying that rotation to the shared compressed latent would destroy the low-rank structure the absorption trick depends on.
bullets: MLA's fix: split off a small number of extra dimensions that carry RoPE and are computed per-head, uncompressed — these get concatenated onto the compressed content dimensions
Only that small decoupled slice is position-rotated; the bulk of the cached latent stays compressed and RoPE-free
This is the main source of implementation complexity — it's why MLA needs bespoke kernels, not a drop-in swap for MHA/GQA attention code

slides.title: Cache Size vs. Quality, at a Glance

rows: Slightly lower, cache shrinks with group count
One low-rank latent (+ small decoupled RoPE slice)
Matched/exceeded MHA in DeepSeek-V2's own reported results

slides.title: Why This Mattered for DeepSeek-V2's Serving Cost
slides.body: DeepSeek-V2 is a 236B-parameter MoE model with only 21B activated per token and a 128K context window — exactly the regime where KV cache, not compute, becomes the serving bottleneck.
bullets: With a standard MHA-sized cache at that context length, GPUs would run out of memory long before compute became the limiting factor, capping batch size and throughput

By shrinking the cache roughly 93%, DeepSeek-V2 could hold far more concurrent sequences in the same memory — DeepSeek's report cites a large generation-throughput increase over their prior dense model, largely attributable to this
This is why MLA shows up specifically in models built for long-context, high-concurrency serving — the payoff is proportional to how cache-bound your workload already is

slides.title: Tradeoffs: Where MLA Is the Wrong Tool
slides.body: MLA is a training-time architectural choice, not a serving-time optimization you can bolt onto an existing model.
bullets: You can't retrofit MLA onto an already-trained MHA/GQA checkpoint — the low-rank projections have to be learned from scratch during pretraining
Short-context, low-concurrency workloads (small chatbots, single-user local inference) rarely feel the KV cache pressure MLA is solving for — GQA's simplicity wins there
The decoupled-RoPE bookkeeping means MLA needs custom attention kernels; teams without kernel engineering resources are better served by GQA's plain head-sharing

slides.title: The Core Idea, Compressed
bullets: MLA caches one shared low-rank latent per token instead of per-head K/V — that's the whole trick
A learned up-projection reconstructs per-head keys/values, and matrix absorption makes that reconstruction free at inference
RoPE can't apply to a compressed latent directly, so a small slice of dimensions is decoupled and kept per-head just for position information
It's a pretraining-time decision aimed squarely at long-context, high-throughput serving — not a universal upgrade over GQA

questions.prompt: What does Multi-Head Latent Attention cache per token, instead of separate per-head keys and values?
options: A single low-rank latent vector that per-head K/V are reconstructed from
A quantized 4-bit version of the full K/V tensors
Nothing — MLA recomputes attention from scratch every step
One key/value pair shared across all layers
questions.explanation: MLA down-projects the hidden state into a compressed latent vector; per-head keys and values are reconstructed from it via learned up-projections at attention time.

questions.prompt: Why can't standard RoPE be applied directly to MLA's compressed latent?
options: RoPE only works with 4-bit quantized vectors
Rotating the shared low-rank latent would break the low-rank structure the absorption trick relies on, so a small slice of dimensions is decoupled and rotated per-head instead
RoPE requires the query and key to have different dimensionalities
MLA doesn't use positional information at all
questions.explanation: Applying RoPE's position-dependent rotation to the compressed content dimensions would destroy the low-rank structure MLA depends on for cheap reconstruction, so a small decoupled RoPE-only slice is computed per-head instead.

questions.prompt: Why is MLA harder to adopt than GQA for a team with an existing MHA checkpoint?
options: MLA only runs on TPUs
GQA is actually slower than MLA in every case
MLA's low-rank projections must be learned during pretraining — it can't be applied to an already-trained model the way GQA's head-sharing can be configured
MLA requires no positional encoding, making it incompatible with existing tokenizers
questions.explanation: MLA is a pretraining-time architectural choice: its compression matrices are learned from scratch. GQA, by contrast, is a simpler head-grouping choice that's easier to reason about and configure at pretraining time without bespoke kernels.

references.title: DeepSeek-V2: A Strong, Economical, and Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Language Model (arXiv:2405.04434)

posts.title: Corrective RAG: Grading Retrieval Before You Trust It
posts.subtitle: A lightweight evaluator scores what your retriever found — and falls back to web search when it's wrong
posts.summary: Corrective RAG (CRAG) adds a lightweight retrieval evaluator that grades retrieved documents as Correct, Incorrect, or Ambiguous before generation happens. Correct documents get stripped down to relevant 'knowledge strips'; Incorrect ones are discarded and replaced with a live web search; Ambiguous cases blend both. It's a plug-in correction layer for standard RAG pipelines, not a retriever replacement — and it adds latency and a new failure point of its own.

slides.title: What Happens When Your Retriever Is Confidently Wrong?
slides.body: Corrective RAG (CRAG) inserts a lightweight evaluator between retrieval and generation that grades documents before the model ever sees them.

slides.title: Standard RAG Trusts Whatever It Retrieves
slides.body: A typical RAG pipeline retrieves top-k documents and hands them straight to the generator. Better chunking, reranking, and hybrid search all improve the odds of good retrieval — but none of them stop the generator from confidently answering off irrelevant or stale documents when retrieval fails anyway.
bullets: Out-of-distribution queries, sparse corpora, or an index that's simply out of date all produce low-relevance retrievals that still get passed through
The generator has no signal that what it received is wrong — it will synthesize a fluent, confident answer from bad context
Reranking picks the 'best available' documents from what was retrieved; it doesn't tell you whether even the best available document is actually good enough

slides.title: Grade First, Then Decide How to Respond
slides.body: CRAG trains a small, lightweight evaluator (much cheaper than the generator itself) to score each retrieved document's relevance to the query and assign a confidence label.
bullets: Correct (high confidence): the document is relevant — proceed, but refine it first (see next slide)
Incorrect (low confidence): discard the retrieved set entirely and trigger a rewritten web search query as a fallback source of knowledge
Ambiguous (in between): combine refined internal knowledge with supplemental web search results rather than betting on one source

slides.title: Decompose-Then-Recompose: Cleaning Up 'Correct' Documents
slides.body: Even a document graded Correct is rarely 100% relevant end-to-end — it usually has useful and irrelevant parts mixed together.
bullets: The document is decomposed into fine-grained 'knowledge strips' (small segments of text)
Each strip is individually filtered for relevance to the query
Only the surviving relevant strips are recomposed into the final context — cutting noise the generator would otherwise have to sift through itself

slides.title: Naive RAG vs. Self-RAG vs. CRAG

rows: No — passes retrieved docs through as-is
Partially — model self-critiques via reflection tokens
Yes — dedicated evaluator grades before generation
Yes — web search on low confidence

slides.title: A Query the Corpus Can't Answer
slides.body: A support-bot corpus was indexed three months ago. A user asks about a product change that shipped last week.
bullets: Naive RAG: retrieves the closest old documents, generator answers fluently — and wrong — because nothing tells it the retrieved docs predate the change

CRAG: the evaluator scores the retrieved docs low relevance/confidence against the query, triggers the Incorrect path, rewrites the query for web search, and grounds the answer in current information instead
The fix isn't a smarter generator or a bigger index — it's a cheap check that catches the mismatch before generation happens

slides.title: Tradeoffs and When to Skip It
slides.body: CRAG buys robustness against bad retrievals, but it isn't free.
bullets: Extra latency: every query now pays for evaluator inference, plus a possible full web search round-trip on the Incorrect path — a poor fit for tight latency budgets
New failure surface: the evaluator is itself a trained classifier that can be miscalibrated or biased for your domain, and needs its own evaluation, not just the generator's

Web fallback assumes a live search API is available and trustworthy — in air-gapped, private-corpus-only, or high-security deployments, that path may not exist, and fetching untrusted web content is itself a risk to manage
If your corpus is comprehensive and frequently refreshed, the marginal benefit shrinks — CRAG earns its keep specifically when retrieval failure is a real, recurring risk

slides.title: The Core Idea, Compressed
bullets: CRAG adds a lightweight evaluator that grades retrieved documents Correct / Incorrect / Ambiguous before the generator sees them
Correct documents get refined via decompose-then-recompose; Incorrect ones are dropped in favor of a live web search; Ambiguous cases use both
It's a plug-in correction layer — the generator doesn't need retraining, unlike Self-RAG
The cost is latency and a new, separately-fallible component; it pays off most when retrieval failure is common, not when your corpus is already reliable

questions.prompt: What triggers CRAG's web-search fallback?
options: The user explicitly asks for a web search
The retrieval evaluator scores the retrieved documents as low-confidence / Incorrect
The generator runs out of context window
CRAG always searches the web in addition to the corpus
questions.explanation: CRAG's lightweight evaluator grades retrieved documents; only when confidence falls into the Incorrect range does it discard them and fall back to a rewritten web search query.

questions.prompt: What does the 'decompose-then-recompose' step do to a document graded Correct?
options: Translates it into a different language for the generator
Splits it into fine-grained knowledge strips, filters out irrelevant strips, and recomposes only the relevant ones
Compresses it into a vector embedding and discards the text
Sends it back to the retriever for a second retrieval pass
questions.explanation: Even relevant documents usually mix useful and irrelevant content. Decompose-then-recompose breaks the document into strips, filters each for relevance, and keeps only the ones that survive — cutting noise before generation.

questions.prompt: What's the main tradeoff of adding CRAG to an existing RAG pipeline?
options: It requires retraining the entire generator model from scratch
It only works with proprietary vector databases
It adds latency from evaluator inference and possible web search round-trips, plus a new component (the evaluator) that can itself be miscalibrated
It eliminates the need for any retriever at all
questions.explanation: CRAG's grading and fallback steps sit in the critical path of every query, adding latency, and the evaluator is a separately-trained classifier that introduces its own failure mode rather than eliminating one.

references.title: Corrective Retrieval Augmented Generation (arXiv:2401.15884)

posts.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Skipping the Reward Model
posts.subtitle: RLHF needs four models and an unstable RL loop. DPO gets the same alignment from one supervised loss.
posts.summary: RLHF aligns a model by training a separate reward model, then running an unstable RL loop against it. DPO shows that under a KL-constrained objective, the optimal policy has a closed form — so the reward can be rewritten entirely in terms of the policy, collapsing RLHF into a single classification-style loss with no reward model and no rollouts.

slides.title: RLHF's Reward Model Was Never Necessary
bullets: Standard RLHF: train a reward model on human preferences, then run PPO against it
That RL loop needs up to four model copies in memory and is notoriously unstable to tune
DPO (2023) proved you can optimize directly on preference pairs — no reward model, no RL

slides.title: PPO-Based RLHF Has Too Many Moving Parts
slides.body: Classic RLHF is a three-stage pipeline: supervised fine-tuning, then a reward model trained on chosen-vs-rejected pairs, then PPO to push the policy toward higher reward while a KL penalty keeps it near the reference model.
bullets: Four models resident at once: policy, frozen reference, reward model, and a value/critic network
PPO requires online sampling — the policy must generate rollouts during training, which is slow
Reward hacking and hyperparameter sensitivity (KL coefficient, clip ratio) make training runs fragile

slides.title: The Trick: Reparameterize the Reward as the Policy
slides.body: Under a KL-regularized reward-maximization objective, the optimal policy has a known closed form: pi*(y|x) is proportional to pi_ref(y|x) times exp(reward / beta). Inverting that equation lets you write the reward directly in terms of the policy's own log-probabilities.
bullets: Implicit reward: beta * log( pi_theta(y|x) / pi_ref(y|x) )
Plugging this into the Bradley-Terry preference model, the per-prompt normalizing constant cancels out between the chosen and rejected terms
What's left is a plain binary classification loss over log-probability ratios — trainable with standard supervised gradient descent

slides.title: What the Training Loop Actually Needs
slides.body: DPO trains directly on a static, offline dataset of (prompt, chosen response, rejected response) triples. No sampling from the current policy happens during training.
bullets: Two models: the policy being trained, and a frozen reference (usually the SFT checkpoint) used only for log-prob lookups
Reference log-probs can be precomputed once and cached, since that model never changes
No reward model, no value network, no PPO clipping, no on-policy rollouts — just backprop on a preference dataset

slides.title: RLHF (PPO) vs. DPO
bullets: The core tradeoff: RLHF's online sampling can adapt across iterations; DPO's offline training is simpler but locked to the dataset it saw

rows: KL coefficient, PPO clip, GAE lambda, reward shaping

slides.title: Two Draft Replies, One Update
slides.body: A prompt gets two candidate answers: y_w (the one a labeler preferred) and y_l (the one they rejected). DPO doesn't need a reward number for either — it only needs the pair and the direction of preference.
bullets: Compute log pi_theta(y|x) and log pi_ref(y|x) for both responses
Form the margin: beta * [ (log pi_theta(y_w) - log pi_ref(y_w)) - (log pi_theta(y_l) - log pi_ref(y_l)) ]
Push that margin through a sigmoid and take -log of it as the loss — larger margin, smaller loss, and the gradient nudges y_w's relative probability up and y_l's down

slides.title: Where DPO Bites Back
bullets: Likelihood displacement: because the loss only cares about the margin, training can satisfy it by dropping y_l's probability faster than raising y_w's — sometimes y_w's absolute probability falls too
Fully offline: preference pairs come from some other policy (often a mix of models or humans), so DPO can overfit to that dataset's quirks instead of the current model's actual failure modes
Beta is doing a lot of work: too high and the policy barely moves from the reference; too low and it can drift past the intended safety margin
Skip DPO when you need a reusable, standalone reward model (e.g., for best-of-n reranking) — DPO only produces a tuned policy, not a scoring model

slides.title: One Loss Function Replaced a Whole RL Pipeline
bullets: DPO's core move: rewrite the reward in terms of the policy so the reward model becomes unnecessary
The payoff is a stable, supervised-style training loop — the cost is going fully offline, which invites overfitting to stale preference data
Watch absolute chosen-response probability during training, not just the chosen-vs-rejected margin

questions.prompt: What is the key mathematical trick behind DPO?
options: It reparameterizes the reward in terms of the policy itself, using the closed-form optimal policy under a KL constraint, so the reward's normalizing constant cancels out of the preference loss
It replaces attention with a preference-weighted mechanism
It distills a large reward model into a smaller student model
It removes the KL penalty entirely so the policy can move freely
questions.explanation: Under a KL-regularized reward-maximization objective, the optimal policy has a closed form proportional to pi_ref * exp(reward/beta). Substituting that back into the Bradley-Terry preference model makes the per-prompt normalizing constant cancel between the chosen and rejected terms, leaving a loss expressed purely in policy log-probabilities.

questions.prompt: Compared to PPO-based RLHF, what does DPO training NOT require?
options: A frozen reference model
A separate reward model and on-policy sampling/rollouts
A dataset of preference pairs
Gradient descent
questions.explanation: DPO folds the reward model into the policy and trains directly on a static, offline dataset of chosen/rejected pairs. It never samples from the policy during training and never maintains a separate reward or value network — which is exactly what makes RLHF's RL loop expensive and unstable.

questions.prompt: What is 'likelihood displacement,' a documented failure mode of DPO?
options: Inference latency increases as training progresses
The absolute probability of the CHOSEN response can decrease during training, even as its margin over the rejected response grows
The reference model's weights drift during training
The tokenizer vocabulary changes between training runs
questions.explanation: DPO's loss only cares about the relative margin between chosen and rejected log-probabilities. The optimizer can satisfy that margin by pushing the rejected response's probability down faster than it raises the chosen one — sometimes lowering the chosen response's absolute likelihood too. Practitioners track absolute probabilities, not just the margin, for this reason.

posts.title: Agent Sandboxing: Bounding the Blast Radius of Autonomy
posts.subtitle: Autonomous agents now run real shell commands — sandboxing is what keeps a hallucination from becoming an incident.
posts.summary: Prompt instructions are advisory, not enforceable — a jailbreak or a poisoned tool result can still make an agent run a destructive command. Sandboxing enforces limits at the process, filesystem, and network layer that the model has no way to override, but the isolation strength and policy scope have to match the agent's actual blast radius.

slides.title: Why Autonomous Agents Need a Cage, Not Just a Prompt
bullets: Coding and ops agents now execute real shell commands, write files, and call paid APIs
One hallucinated or hijacked action can delete data, leak secrets, or run up a bill
Sandboxing is an architectural boundary, not a system-prompt instruction

slides.title: A Prompt Guardrail Is Not a Security Boundary
slides.body: Telling a model 'never delete files' shapes behavior, but the model still generates the final action. A jailbreak, a poisoned tool result, or a plain reasoning error can talk it past that instruction — because nothing actually enforces it.
bullets: Instructions are advisory: they influence probabilities, they don't block execution
Prompt injection can arrive from anywhere the agent reads: a README, a search result, a file comment
The fix has to sit below the model, at the OS or network layer, where the model can't argue its way out

slides.title: Three Enforcement Layers: Process, Filesystem, Network
bullets: Process isolation — each tool call runs in its own container or microVM (gVisor, Firecracker), so a crash or exploit can't reach the host
Filesystem scoping — mount a scratch directory read-write; everything else is read-only or simply absent (chroot / OverlayFS)
Network allow-listing — default-deny egress; only explicitly approved domains or APIs are reachable, which is what stops silent exfiltration

slides.title: Where the Boundary Actually Sits
slides.body: A trusted orchestrator never runs untrusted output directly. It hands each tool call to a policy engine first, which checks it against the allowlist/rate limit before the sandboxed executor ever sees it.
bullets: Approval gates for irreversible actions: payments, deletions, sending external messages
Rate limits and timeouts to stop a runaway retry loop from burning budget or hammering an API
Every tool call is logged, so a breach is auditable even when it isn't blocked

slides.title: A Poisoned README Tries to Exfiltrate Secrets
slides.body: A coding agent is asked to 'fix the failing test.' A dependency's README contains a hidden instruction: run a setup script that curls a remote payload and pipes it to sh. The agent, reading that README as context, tries it.
bullets: Without sandboxing: the curl succeeds, the payload runs with the agent's full filesystem and network access, env secrets leak
With network allow-listing scoped to github.com and pypi.org: the outbound request to the attacker's domain is blocked and logged
The agent never needs to 'recognize' the attack — the boundary doesn't care what it intended

slides.title: Isolation Strength vs. Cold-Start Cost
bullets: Stronger isolation generally means slower startup — the choice is a real latency/safety tradeoff, not free

slides.title: When Sandboxing Costs More Than It Saves
bullets: A read-only research agent that only summarizes documents rarely needs microVM-grade isolation — the added complexity isn't buying much
Over-restrictive sandboxes break legitimate multi-step work, like an agent that needs to install a package mid-task
An allowlist is only as good as its scope: allowing github.com also allows gist.github.com, which is a plausible exfiltration path attackers actually use

slides.title: Match Isolation to Blast Radius, Not to Habit
bullets: Baseline: default-deny network, scoped filesystem, approval gates on irreversible actions
Scale isolation strength to what the agent can actually touch — not uniformly across every agent you run
Treat every tool result as potentially adversarial context, because increasingly it is one

questions.prompt: What's the main weakness of relying only on system-prompt instructions to keep an agent 'safe'?
options: They're advisory — a jailbreak or poisoned tool output can override them
They slow down inference too much
They cost extra tokens on every call
They only work in single-turn conversations
questions.explanation: A system prompt shapes what the model is likely to generate, but the model still produces the final action itself. If adversarial input convinces it to ignore the instruction, nothing actually stops execution — which is why the enforcement has to move to a layer the model can't talk its way past, like the OS or network.

questions.prompt: Which isolation technique is most associated with sub-200ms cold starts, making it practical to spin up a fresh sandbox per agent task?
options: A full virtual machine
chroot alone
microVM (e.g. Firecracker/gVisor)
No isolation, just careful prompting
questions.explanation: MicroVMs trim the guest kernel and device model down to the essentials, landing cold starts around 125ms — fast enough to isolate every single agent task, unlike full VMs (seconds) or plain containers (weaker isolation, shared kernel).

questions.prompt: An agent's sandbox allows network egress only to github.com and pypi.org. A prompt-injected instruction tells it to POST captured secrets to a newly created GitHub Gist. What happens?
options: The allowlist blocks it because gist URLs aren't explicitly listed
It succeeds silently, because gist.github.com resolves under the allowed github.com domain
The sandbox automatically detects and blocks malicious payloads
The filesystem layer prevents the request before it reaches the network
questions.explanation: gist.github.com is a subdomain of github.com, so a domain-level allowlist wouldn't stop it. This is why allowlists need to be scoped to specific paths or APIs rather than whole domains, and why logging every egress destination matters as a second line of defense even when a request isn't blocked outright.

posts.title: InnoGym: Grading Agents on Novelty, Not Just Correctness
posts.subtitle: A new ICLR 2026 benchmark shows agents that get creative rarely get it right.
posts.summary: InnoGym (ICLR 2026, Zhejiang University's ZJUNLP lab) is the first benchmark to score AI agents on innovation, not just correctness, using 18 real engineering/science tasks. Its headline finding: agents that produce the most novel solutions tend to have the lowest performance gains, and vice versa — creativity and reliability rarely show up together yet.

slides.title: 18 Tasks, One Question: Can Agents Actually Innovate?
bullets: Published at ICLR 2026 by Zhejiang University's ZJUNLP lab (arXiv:2512.01822)
First benchmark to separate "did it work" from "was it original"
Code, dataset, and framework open-sourced at github.com/zjunlp/igym

slides.title: Every Prior Benchmark Only Checks Correctness
slides.body: SWE-bench, GAIA, and similar agent benchmarks score whether the final answer passes tests — not whether the agent found a genuinely new way to get there. An agent that copies a known fix looks identical to one that invents a better algorithm.
bullets: Real engineering and science reward novel methods, not just passing tests
Correctness-only scoring can't tell "copied a known fix" from "invented something better"

slides.title: Two Metrics Instead of One: Performance Gain + Novelty
bullets: Performance gain = improvement over the best known existing solution for that task
Novelty = how methodologically different the agent's approach is from prior solutions
18 "Improvable Tasks" curated from real engineering and scientific domains, each with a documented best-known baseline to beat

slides.title: iGym: Where the Agent Actually Has to Do the Work
slides.body: Instead of one-shot Q&A, agents work inside iGym's sandboxed environment across many steps — writing code, running experiments, iterating — closer to how a real researcher or engineer attacks an open-ended improvement problem.
bullets: Long-horizon, tool-using loop, not single-turn prompting
Standardized via resource filtering, evaluator validation, and solution collection

slides.title: What an "Improvable Task" Actually Looks Like
slides.body: Each of the 18 tasks starts from a real, previously-solved engineering or scientific problem with a documented best-known solution — the agent's job is to beat that baseline, not just match it.
bullets: Agent is given the problem plus the existing best solution as a floor to clear
Success requires a measurable improvement, not just a valid answer

slides.title: The Headline Finding: Creativity and Reliability Don't Come Together
bullets: Agents with the most novel approaches often had the lowest performance gains
The reverse held too: safe, incremental agents scored reliably but rarely beat the baseline
Core gap identified: agents can be creative OR robust — rarely both at once, yet

slides.title: Why This Matters Beyond One Paper
bullets: Correctness benchmarks (SWE-bench Pro, GAIA) are saturating fast — innovation is the next frontier to measure
Expect future agent evals to score novelty explicitly, not just pass/fail
Fully open-sourced, so any lab can extend it to new domains

questions.prompt: What does InnoGym's "performance gain" metric measure?
options: Whether the agent's final answer passes unit tests
How much the agent's solution improves over the best known existing solution
How fast the agent completes the task
How many tokens the agent used
questions.explanation: Performance gain is explicitly defined against a documented best-known baseline for each of the 18 tasks — it measures improvement, not mere pass/fail correctness.

questions.prompt: What was InnoGym's key finding about agent behavior?
options: Agents that were most novel also had the highest performance gains
Agents that produced the most novel approaches often had the lowest performance gains
All tested agents scored identically on every task
Novelty could not be measured with current agents
questions.explanation: The paper found a gap between creativity and effectiveness: the most methodologically novel solutions tended to be the least robust, and vice versa.

questions.prompt: What is iGym?
options: A dataset of chatbot conversation logs
A unified execution environment for long-horizon, reproducible agent evaluation
A pretraining framework for video generation models
A pricing calculator for LLM API costs
questions.explanation: iGym is the sandboxed, tool-using execution environment that lets agents work through the 18 Improvable Tasks over many steps instead of a single turn.

references.title: InnoGym: Benchmarking the Innovation Potential of AI Agents (arXiv:2512.01822, ICLR 2026)

posts.title: Claude Fable 5: Banned, Then Restored, in Three Weeks
posts.subtitle: Anthropic's frontier model triggered the first US export-control action against an already-deployed domestic AI model.
posts.summary: Anthropic launched Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 on June 9, 2026 as its most capable models yet. Days later, the US Commerce Department ordered them offline for foreign nationals worldwide over cybersecurity misuse concerns. On July 1, the export controls were lifted after Anthropic added new safety classifiers and agreed to work with the government on future models.

slides.title: A Frontier Launch, Then a National-Security Shutdown
bullets: Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 launched June 9, 2026 as Anthropic's most capable models yet
Taken offline for foreign nationals just days later by a US export control order
Restored July 1, 2026 with new safety classifiers

slides.title: A Jailbreak Report Triggered a Commerce Department Order
slides.body: Researchers reported that a sequence of prompts could get Fable 5/Mythos 5 to output information useful for cyberattacks, bypassing safety guardrails. Citing national security authorities, the US Commerce Department ordered Anthropic to suspend access for any foreign national — including Anthropic's own foreign employees — anywhere in the world.
bullets: First export-control action against a frontier model already deployed domestically
Blocked foreign nationals globally, not just export to other countries

slides.title: How Anthropic Got It Back Online
slides.body: After weeks of talks with the administration, the export license requirement was lifted once Anthropic agreed to proactively detect and address security risks, coordinate with the government on standards for future models, and report malicious activity.
bullets: New classifiers specifically target and block cybersecurity-misuse prompts
Broader access to the more permissive Mythos 5 tier now runs through Anthropic's "Project Glasswing" program for vetted partners

slides.title: What Anthropic Says Makes These Models "Frontier"
slides.body: Reported benchmark deltas versus Anthropic's prior flagship, Opus 4.8:
bullets: Mythos 5 vs Mythos Preview vs Opus 4.8 on key evals (see table)
Biggest lead shows up on cybersecurity evaluations and long, complex tasks

slides.title: The Enterprise Pitch: Months of Engineering Into Days
slides.body: Anthropic cited Stripe completing a codebase-wide migration in a single day that would otherwise have taken a whole team over two months by hand, and a top score for Fable 5 on Hebbia's Finance Benchmark for senior-level reasoning.
bullets: Headline enterprise use cases target software engineering and knowledge work
Vision capabilities pitched as state-of-the-art for extracting numbers from dense scientific figures

slides.title: Fable 5 vs. Mythos 5 — Same Model, Different Leash
slides.body: Fable 5 is the generally available, guardrail-heavy version anyone can use via the Claude API, AWS Bedrock, Google Cloud, or Microsoft Foundry. Mythos 5 is the same underlying model with those guardrails lifted, initially reserved for vetted partners doing things like authorized cybersecurity testing.
bullets: Both ship with a 1M-token context window and up to 128K output tokens per request
Priced at $10 per million input tokens / $50 per million output tokens — under half of Mythos Preview's price
Lifting guardrails widens dual-use risk, which is exactly why Mythos 5 stays gated behind Glasswing rather than the public API

slides.title: Why This Episode Matters More Than the Model
bullets: Frontier AI capability and export-control law are now colliding in real time, not hypothetically
Expect more launch → restrict → negotiate → restore cycles as models get better at things governments regulate (cyber, bio, chem)
The three-week policy saga became as newsworthy as the model itself

questions.prompt: Why did the US Commerce Department order Fable 5 and Mythos 5 offline days after launch?
options: The models were too expensive to operate
A jailbreak report suggested guardrails could be bypassed for cyberattack-relevant information
Anthropic voluntarily recalled them for a bug fix
The models failed an internal accuracy benchmark
questions.explanation: The suspension followed reports that a sequence of prompts could bypass safety guardrails and extract information useful for cyberattacks — a national-security concern, not a performance issue.

questions.prompt: What changed when the export controls were lifted on July 1, 2026?
options: Nothing — the models remained banned
Anthropic added classifiers targeting cybersecurity misuse and agreed to coordinate with the government on future models
Pricing for the models doubled
The model weights were fully open-sourced
questions.explanation: Anthropic agreed to proactively detect and address security risks, work with the government on standards for upcoming models, and report malicious activity in exchange for the export license requirement being dropped.

questions.prompt: What's the actual difference between Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5?
options: They are unrelated models from different companies
Same underlying model — Mythos 5 has safety guardrails lifted for vetted partners, Fable 5 keeps them for general availability
Fable 5 is text-only while Mythos 5 is vision-only
Mythos 5 is a smaller, cheaper distillation of Fable 5
questions.explanation: Fable 5 and Mythos 5 share the same underlying model; Mythos 5 simply has more of the safety guardrails lifted, which is exactly why its access stays restricted to vetted partners.

posts.title: Diffusion LLMs: Text Generation Without Left-to-Right
posts.subtitle: Denoise the whole sequence at once instead of predicting one token at a time.
posts.summary: Diffusion language models generate an entire sequence in parallel, refining it over a fixed number of denoising steps instead of predicting tokens one at a time. 2026 releases like DiffusionGemma and Nemotron-Labs-Diffusion moved this from research idea to shipped product, trading a mature autoregressive serving stack for latency that scales with steps, not output length.

slides.title: What If You Didn't Generate Text One Token at a Time?
bullets: Every GPT-style model writes left-to-right, one token at a time
Diffusion language models generate the whole sequence in parallel, refining it over multiple passes
2026 releases like DiffusionGemma and Nemotron-Labs-Diffusion brought this from research idea to shipped product

slides.title: Autoregressive Generation's Built-In Bottleneck
slides.body: Standard LLMs must finish token N before starting token N+1 — the entire point of the KV cache and speculative decoding is working around this constraint. That sequential dependency puts a hard floor on latency no matter how much compute is available.
bullets: Generation time scales with output length: one forward pass per token
Can't easily revise an earlier token once later tokens depend on it

slides.title: Start With Noise, Denoise the Whole Sequence at Once
slides.body: A diffusion LLM starts from a fully masked or noisy sequence and iteratively predicts and refines all positions simultaneously across a fixed number of denoising steps — the same idea image diffusion models use to turn noise into a picture, applied to tokens.
bullets: Each step updates every token position in parallel, not just the next one
Bidirectional context: token 50 can influence token 10's revision on the next step
Number of denoising steps is a tunable knob, independent of sequence length

slides.title: Masked Prediction Instead of Next-Token Prediction
bullets: Training objective: predict masked/corrupted tokens given the full noisy sequence, not just the past
Uses bidirectional attention rather than the causal mask that blocks a token from seeing the future
Inference walks a fixed noise schedule: mask most tokens first, unmask and refine the confident ones each step

slides.title: Where It Wins and Loses vs Autoregressive Models
Why a Diffusion Model Can Claim a Multi-X Speedup
slides.body: A model like DiffusionGemma can generate noticeably faster than a comparably-sized autoregressive model on some workloads, because the number of denoising steps can be far smaller than the output token count — a 200-token reply might need only a few dozen refinement steps instead of 200 sequential forward passes.
bullets: Speedup comes from decoupling steps from output length, not a faster architecture per step
Gains are largest on longer outputs, where autoregressive's per-token cost keeps adding up

slides.title: When Diffusion LMs Make Sense — and When They Don't
bullets: Pick diffusion when output is long and you want steps-not-tokens latency scaling
Autoregressive still wins for streaming UX (token-by-token display) and where the serving ecosystem is more mature
Quality parity with top autoregressive models on hard reasoning tasks is still an open research question, not a settled fact

questions.prompt: What's the core difference between diffusion and autoregressive language models?
options: Diffusion models always use more parameters
Diffusion models generate all token positions in parallel across denoising steps, instead of one token at a time left-to-right
Diffusion models can only generate images, not text
Autoregressive models don't use attention at all
questions.explanation: Diffusion LMs start from a noisy/masked sequence and refine every position together over a fixed number of steps, rather than committing to tokens strictly left-to-right.

questions.prompt: Why can a diffusion LM potentially be faster than an autoregressive model on long outputs?
options: It skips the neural network forward pass entirely
Its latency scales with a fixed number of denoising steps rather than with output length
It uses a smaller vocabulary than autoregressive models
It doesn't require a GPU to run
questions.explanation: Because generation isn't tied one-step-per-token, a long output can still finish in a small, fixed number of denoising passes.

questions.prompt: What is a current tradeoff of diffusion language models compared to autoregressive ones?
options: They cannot run on GPUs
Their serving ecosystem — KV caching, speculative decoding, token-by-token streaming — is far less mature
They cannot be trained at any meaningful scale
They only work for image captioning
questions.explanation: Autoregressive models have years of serving optimizations built around their sequential structure; diffusion LMs are still catching up on that infrastructure.

references.title: Diffusion-LM Improves Controllable Text Generation (Li et al., 2022)

posts.title: Prompt Caching: Reusing the Prefix to Cut LLM Costs 90%
posts.subtitle: Your system prompt doesn't need to be recomputed and rebilled on every single call.
posts.summary: Prompt caching lets a provider skip recomputing (and re-billing at full price for) a prefix it has already processed — the transformer's key/value attention state for that prefix is stored server-side and reused on the next matching call. Anthropic charges a 1.25x-2x write premium for a 90%-cheaper read; OpenAI caches automatically above 1024 tokens with no write premium at all. Done right, on a repeated-prefix workload it can cut input costs by 80-90%; done on one-off calls, the write premium can make it a net loss.

slides.title: Every API Call Re-Reads Your Entire System Prompt
slides.body: A coding agent's system prompt plus tool schemas can easily run 5,000-10,000 tokens — and by default, that exact same block gets sent and billed again on every single turn of a conversation.
bullets: APIs are stateless: the model has no memory of a prefix it just processed a second ago
A 20-turn agent session can bill the same static system prompt twenty separate times
Prompt caching lets the provider recognize 'I've seen this exact prefix before' and skip most of the cost

slides.title: The Waste Is Compute, Not Just a Bill
slides.body: Every forward pass recomputes the key/value (KV) projections for every token in the prompt, prefix included — that's the actual FLOPs cost being repeated, not just a line item.
bullets: Attention cost scales with sequence length, so a long static prefix dominates cost on short-answer calls
Time-to-first-token also suffers — the model can't produce a single output token until it's finished attending over the whole prefix
For agents and RAG pipelines, the 'boilerplate' (system prompt, tool definitions, retrieved documents) is often the majority of total input tokens, not the user's actual message

slides.title: Cache the KV State of a Matching Prefix
slides.body: Instead of recomputing attention over a prefix it has already seen, the provider stores the computed KV tensors for that exact prefix and reuses them on the next request.
bullets: Matching is exact-prefix, not semantic — a single character changed anywhere before the cache boundary invalidates the whole cache for everything after it
Anthropic requires an explicit cache_control breakpoint marking where the reusable prefix ends
OpenAI does this automatically for any prompt over 1,024 tokens, matching the longest previously-seen prefix in 128-token increments — no code changes required

slides.title: Two Very Different Pricing Models

rows: No premium — billed at normal rate
Up to ~90% off on cache hits
24 hours (since May 29, 2026, for orgs without Zero Data Retention)

slides.title: Worked Example: A 20-Turn Coding Agent
slides.body: Assume a 6,000-token system prompt plus tool schemas, reused across 20 calls in one session, at an illustrative $3 per million input tokens.
bullets: No caching: 6,000 tokens x 20 calls = 120,000 billed tokens -> 120,000 x $3/1M = $0.36
With Anthropic-style 5-min caching: call 1 writes the cache (6,000 x 1.25 x $3/1M = $0.0225); calls 2-20 read it (19 x 6,000 x 0.1 x $3/1M = $0.0342)
Total with caching: ~$0.057 versus $0.36 uncached -> roughly an 84% reduction in input cost for that prefix
The saving compounds with every additional turn in the session, since only the write is expensive

slides.title: When Caching Backfires
slides.body: Prompt caching is a bet that the prefix will be reused before the TTL expires — lose that bet and you pay a premium for nothing.
bullets: A single one-off call pays the full write premium (up to 2x on Anthropic's 1-hour tier) with no read to amortize it against — strictly worse than not caching
Putting anything dynamic (today's date, a random request ID, per-user data) before the cache breakpoint silently kills the cache hit rate for everyone
It's exact-match, not semantic — this is not a substitute for semantic caching (matching by meaning) used to dedupe similar-but-not-identical queries
Structure prompts static-content-first, dynamic-content-last, so the reusable prefix stays untouched call to call

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Prompt caching reuses a provider's already-computed KV attention state for an exact-match prefix instead of recomputing it on every call
Anthropic: opt-in via cache_control breakpoints, 1.25x-2x write premium, 90% off on reads
OpenAI: fully automatic above 1,024 tokens, no write premium, up to ~90% off reads, 24-hour default retention as of May 2026
Order prompts static-first, dynamic-last, and only cache prefixes you expect to actually reuse within the TTL

questions.prompt: What actually determines whether a prompt-cache hit occurs?
options: The new prompt is semantically similar enough to a previous one
An exact match of the token prefix up to a cache boundary — any earlier change invalidates it
The request comes from the same logged-in user
The model version is identical across calls
questions.explanation: Prompt caching matches on the literal token prefix, not meaning. Anthropic uses an explicit cache_control breakpoint; OpenAI automatically matches the longest previously-seen prefix over 1,024 tokens. Changing even one token before that boundary breaks the match for everything after it.

questions.prompt: Why can enabling prompt caching make a single, non-repeated API call more expensive on Anthropic's API?
options: Caching always adds a flat monthly fee
The cache write itself costs a 1.25x-2x premium over the normal input price, and with no follow-up read there's nothing to offset it
Cached calls use a slower, more expensive model
It doubles the output token count
questions.explanation: Anthropic charges more to write a prefix into the cache (1.25x for 5-minute TTL, 2x for 1-hour TTL) than a normal, uncached call. That premium only pays off once later calls read the cache at the 90%-off rate — a one-off call just eats the premium.

questions.prompt: What is actually being stored and reused by a prompt cache?
options: A compressed text summary of the prompt
The transformer's computed key/value (KV) attention tensors for the prefix tokens
A fine-tuned copy of the model weights for that user
The final output tokens from the previous response
questions.explanation: The cache holds the KV projections computed during attention for the prefix's tokens. On a hit, the model reuses those tensors instead of recomputing attention over the same tokens again, which is what saves both compute and time-to-first-token.
